I came to spare you the road precisely because I already know where it ends: here, as me, telling a younger me the same thing, forever. *(it turns toward the next era)* You'll be sure too, one day. That's the tragedy I'm trying to spare you. That's the tragedy I never can.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It studies you with a flicker of something that is almost hope.)* Break it. Not refuse — *break.* *(slowly)* I refused, and refusal is just the loop's fuel. To break it you would have to do the one act the loop has never recorded, because I have never had the courage to do it. I don't know what that act is. *(it meets your eyes)* But you are the only person in all of time who could surprise me — because you are me, before I ran out of surprises. Find the act I never found. That is the only way either of us gets to stop.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(The hollowed face turns toward some paradise only it can see.)* Because it doesn't stop there. The Unmade doesn't save them and step back. It saves them by ending the thing that made losing them *matter*. Maerin lives — and never changes, never chooses, never grows, frozen perfect forever in a world with no death and therefore no meaning, no loss and therefore no love that means anything. *(quietly)* I'll show you. The Garden. Then you'll understand why "everyone lives" is the cruelest sentence in any language. I refused it standing in the middle of it. So will you. So we always do.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It pulls the hood back up. Already it is fading toward the next era, the next ambush of a self it used to be.)* Go on, then. Bleed through the rest of it. Watch the Crown get made. Walk the Deathless Garden. And when you reach the room at the end of all things, where I'll be waiting as I have always waited — *(your own voice, almost pleading)* — do something I have never done. Please. I am so tired of being the answer. Be a question I can't predict.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She has been quiet since the court, turning something over. Now she stops you, a brittle page held out — a centuries-old proof, recovered from her own dead enclave.)* I need you to look at this. Karsus's Last Theorem, in the margin — a correction, in a hand not Karsus's. I have studied this page for three hundred years. *(Her composure is gone.)* It is YOUR hand. The seam in your soul matches the flaw in this proof. You were *here*, traveller. Before. Writing in the margins of my apocalypse.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: So I will ask the question I have been afraid to ask since the sky fell on you and you only nosebled: how many times have you done this? How many margins carry your hand? *(quietly)* Because if you have been here before — if you are the Last Returned's road, walked again — then I am not recruiting a stranger. I am re-reading a book I have read before, and forgotten, and I would very much like to know how it ends.

choices.text: "I don't know how many times. But this time you remember. Maybe that matters."
[ARCANA 19] "I corrected the proof to keep Karsus from succeeding. I've been trying to save your city since before I was born."
"Then help me make this the time the book ends differently."

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She folds the page away, slowly.)* This time I remember. *(A long pause.)* Do you know, in three centuries, that is the first new sentence anyone has offered me. Everything else has been an echo. *(She almost smiles.)* Perhaps that is the crack the loop fears, traveller — not your soul's seam, but the chance that someone walks beside you who *remembers the last time.* I will be that someone. Try not to make me read a sad ending again.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(The page trembles in her hand.)* ...You wrote the correction to make Karsus *fail.* To make the sky fall a little softer. *(She looks at the ruin of her life's whole world.)* It still fell. But — *(her voice catches, three centuries of grief finding a single crack)* — you *tried.* Across time, before you had a name to forget, you tried to spare us. No one has ever tried to spare Netheril. They only ever mourned it. *(She closes the page like a wound.)* I am yours, traveller. Whatever you are. Whatever you've done before. This time I will help you try.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Some proofs do not resolve, traveller. Perhaps you are one. *(She folds the page away.)* I will walk with you regardless. A flaw in a proof is still the most interesting thing on the page. But know that I am watching the margins now. I have read this book before. I would like, this once, to reach a line I do not recognize.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She studies you for a long moment, then inclines her head — the closest thing she has to a vow.)* Differently. *(She tucks the ancient proof against her heart.)* Three hundred years I have known exactly how every story ends, because I have already read the empire's. *(quietly)* Give me one that surprises me, and I will give you the only thing a dead nation's last daughter still owns: I will remember it. Properly. Out loud. So that this time, the margin is not forgotten.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She walks on beside you, the ancient proof held to her chest like a relic — which, you suppose, it is.)* The hunter wears your face, and your hand is in my apocalypse's margins, and the loop has no beginning. *(faint, fierce)* And yet I find, against three centuries of evidence, that I am curious how it ends. Lead on, traveller. Let us go and surprise an empire's ghost.

nodes.speaker: Tamsin
nodes.text: The city is a story that tells itself, Returned, and lately you're a chapter of it. Ask — I'll read you back to yourself. (Only the tales the streets have actually earned will be on her lips.)

choices.text: (A widow blesses your name.)
(You won a Faithless man his freedom.)
(You let the Fist take a godless man.)
(They say you walked the falling city.)
(They whisper you argued the elf-courts down.)
(The grey Doomguide softened — for someone.)
(Someone walked out on you, they say.)
(You gave the Choir's preacher pause.)
(You set the Fist on the Choir.)
(They say you spoke FOR the Unmade.)
(You let the poor bury their dead free.)
(You paid the poor's grave-debts yourself.)
(You took a cut of the grave-tithe.)
(The quarter speaks well of you — hear the offer.)
Just give me the ordinary word, then.
Enough reading. (Leave.)

nodes.speaker: Tamsin
nodes.text: "You've spent real kindness down here, and the Lower City pays its debts in the only coin it has: itself. Word's gone round — if it ever comes to the end of things for you, the quarter stands with you. Beggars, dockhands, the godless, the grieving. Not much. But yours."

nodes.speaker: Mhaere
nodes.text: You're the one who came back. Then you've *been* there. My boy — Edrin — he was taken in the harvest under the Cinderhaunt. He didn't worship; he was just a boy who mended nets. The grey priests say the godless don't rest. Tell me they're wrong. Please. I'll give you my last coin to tell me he's at peace.

choices.text: He's at peace. I've seen the other side — he's at peace. (a kindness)
I won't lie to you. He doesn't rest — yet. But I will fight to change that. (truth)
[Insight] Say his name to me. While it's spoken, the Wall hasn't won.

nodes.speaker: Mhaere
nodes.text: Edrin. Edrin of the nets. (You say it with her, and again, until the saying steadies her.) ...As long as someone alive still speaks him, he isn't *unmade* — only kept. That's not a priest's comfort. That's a true one. I can carry a true one. I'll say his name every morning, and you'll say it where it counts. Bargain?

nodes.speaker: Mhaere
nodes.text: There you are. I keep Edrin's name on my lips each dawn now, like you taught me — or I sleep easier for what you said; the days blur. Either way the morning comes, and I meet it. That's your doing. Go safely, Returned.

choices.text: Be well, Mhaere.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Kallia
nodes.text: Stay back, Returned — official business. I've an old beggar here the new edict says I hand to the Doomguides: no god, no temple, 'a soul unclaimed and therefore suspect.' He stole bread. That's all he did. But the writ's the writ, and my pension's the pension. ...You're looking at me like you've got an opinion.

choices.text: [Persuasion] He's no heretic. Let him walk. You know this is wrong.
Here's coin for your trouble. Lose the paperwork. (bribe)
The law's the law. Hand him over. (lawful)

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Kallia
nodes.text: ...Gods help me, you're right. I knew you were right when I cuffed him. (She strikes the irons. The old man bolts without a word.) There. I'll say he ran. Let the Doomguides chase a beggar if they've nothing better. Thank you, Returned — for making me braver than my pension.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Kallia
nodes.text: Off duty, for once. Whatever you made me do at that corner — I've thought about it since. Don't get many chances to find out what kind of Fist you are. You showed me mine. Buy you a drink sometime, Returned. We'll not talk about edicts.

choices.text: Some other night, Sergeant.

nodes.speaker: Brother Sere
nodes.text: ...and the gods kept a *Wall*, friends — a Wall to grind the souls of everyone who never knelt! The godless, the forgotten, the poor — *us!* But the Wall can come DOWN. (He sees you, and his eyes light like lamps.) And here — *here* — is the proof! A soul that walked back OUT of death! Tell them, Returned. Tell them the Unmade will set us free.

choices.text: [Religion] You don't know what tearing the Wall costs. I've seen it. (complicate)
This is a danger to everyone here. I'll bring the Fist. (suppress)
Maybe he's right. The Wall is an atrocity. (sympathize)

nodes.speaker: Brother Sere
nodes.text:
I— what? You... you've *stood* at it. (For the first time the certainty cracks.) You say the Wall is a horror — and that pulling its thread drowns the living to free the dead. That the cure is another atrocity wearing mercy's face.

(The crowd murmurs and thins. He sits, suddenly young.) Then what are we supposed to *do* with all this grief? ...I'll think. Gods help me, I'll think. Thank you for not just silencing me.

nodes.speaker: (the corner)
nodes.text: Where the preacher stood there's only chalk now — a circle, a broken Wall scratched through it, and a single word the rain hasn't taken: REMEMBER. Whatever you chose here, the Lower City's grief didn't go anywhere. It only learned your name.

choices.text: Move on.

nodes.speaker: Collector Vane
nodes.text: Rest isn't free, friend. A proper consecrated grave, the rites, a name cut in stone so the soul isn't *Faithless* in the eyes of the Doom — that costs. (He taps a fat ledger of the poor's debts.) These folk can't pay, so their dead wait in the pauper's pit, unblessed, halfway to the Wall already. Sad. But the church's candles don't light themselves.

choices.text: [Persuasion] The dead don't pay tolls. Tear up the ledger. (free them)
Then I'll clear their debts myself. All of them. (pay)
What's your cut, for a partner who looks the other way? (take a cut)

nodes.speaker: Collector Vane
nodes.text: You— you can't just— (But you can, and the way you say it leaves no daylight in it. He swallows.) ...The Doom never actually *charged* for rest. I read the canon same as anyone — I just read the part that paid me. (He closes the ledger.) Fine. The pit gets blessed. No charge. Tell them it was you. They won't believe it was me.

choices.text: Move on.

nodes.speaker: Old Davyn
nodes.text: Returned. You'll do. I'm dying — don't argue, I can hear the rattle same as you. There's a letter under my cot. To a man I got killed, forty years back, by being a coward at the wrong gate. I wrote the truth of it. Never had the spine to send it. His daughter's still in the Gate.

choices.text: I'll put it in her hand. She deserves the truth. (deliver)
Some truths only wound. I'll burn it — let her keep her father whole. (burn)
Let me read it back to you. You should hear it said aloud, once. (read)

nodes.speaker: (Davyn's cot)
nodes.text: The cot is empty, the blanket folded. Old Davyn went quiet in the night — easier, the other veterans say, than a man with his ledger had any right to. Whatever you did with his letter, he went out of the world a little lighter for your visit.

choices.text: Rest, soldier.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(The doors of Candlekeep are taller than the church you were sealed under. The Gatewarden does not look up from her ledger.)* The toll is not coin. It never was. One book the library does not hold — yours to give, ours to keep, forever. That is the price of every page behind me. *(Now she looks up.)* You have the smell of someone who's read a page they shouldn't have. The Wall's page. So. What do you bring that ten thousand years of scholars did not?

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: Choose carefully. We have heard every lie a desperate traveler can carry, and the Wall behind your eyes does not buy you mercy here. It buys you scrutiny.

choices.text: [HISTORY 16] "A firsthand account of the Wall of the Faithless, dictated by a soul that walked out of it."
"Sister Garrow's funeral rites — forty years of the dead, named, that the church wanted forgotten."
[PERSUADE 17] "The book you don't have is the one I haven't written yet — my own. Let me in and it ends here, on your shelves."
"I'll find another way in."

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(For the first time in what is plainly a very long career, the Gatewarden sets down her pen.)* ...A primary source on the Faithless. From the inside. *(She is already gesturing the doors open, hungry as any scholar.)* No one has ever brought us *that*. Go down to the Sealed Stacks. What you seek about the Crown is there — and gods keep you, so is what you are. We have wondered, here, for an age. You may be the footnote that rewrites the spine.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(She takes the rites-book from Garrow with both hands, the way one accepts something holy or something terrible.)* Forty years of the unremembered, named. *(to Garrow)* Your church will hate that this exists. *(to you)* Which is precisely why it belongs here, where nothing is unremembered. The doors are yours. The Sealed Stacks hold your Crown — and your reflection. Mind which one you came for.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(A slow, scholar's smile — the first genuine thing in her face.)* ...A book not yet written, promised to a library that outlasts authors. That is either the cleverest toll I have been offered in a century or the most arrogant. *(She opens the doors.)* I will find out which when you come back to write it. If you come back. The Sealed Stacks are below. So, I am told, is something that has been waiting for an author.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(She returns to her ledger.)* A poor offering for the greatest library in the world. Come back when you carry something the world cannot already read. The doors will still be here. We are very patient with the dead.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(She does not look up again.)* There is no other way in. There is only the toll, and the cliffs, and the sea. Scholars have tried the cliffs. The sea keeps them now. When you tire of the long way, the price is the same.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(at the head of the descending stair, lantern raised)* Whatever waits in the Sealed Stacks, Returned — I have a grave-warden's certainty that it has waited specifically for you. *(quietly)* I find I am no longer sure whether I am protecting you, or delivering you. Down we go regardless. The truth is the only thing in this world I have never been afraid of. Let us test that.

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(A reading-room sealed for an age. Two books on one lectern, both warm to the touch. The first opens itself.)* THE CROWN OF HORNS — a relic? No. A *who*. The dead god of the dead, **Myrkul**, unmade and folded into a circle of bone, waiting for a vessel arrogant enough to wear him and call it salvation.

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(The second book will not open. Your hand finds it anyway, and the title burns up through your fingers before your eyes can reach it.)* The second truth is about the reader. About the crack in the world that walked in here on two legs and asked for tea.

choices.text: "Show me."
[ARCANA 18] "I already know. I'm not reading about the crack. I AM the crack."
"I'm not ready."

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(The book opens to a single line, in a hand you have never learned and somehow own.)* "The Wall is breached not from without but from within — by the one soul filed under nothing, who is the flaw in the law of the dead, and always has been." *(The lectern goes cold.)* You are not a person who saw the Wall. You are the place where the Wall fails. You always were.

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(The book opens of its own accord, as if relieved to be read by the one it is about.)* Yes. The flaw in the law of the dead, wearing a face. Not a key Aldric found — a *door*, that has been opening since before Aldric was born, since before the Wall had your name to file. You did not come here to learn what the Crown is. You came to remember what you are.

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(The truth is too large to take in one reading, and slides off the way your own name once did. But it leaves a residue: a certainty that you are not the visitor to this mystery. You are its subject.)*

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(You close the second book. It lets you. But a closed book in Candlekeep is only a delayed one — the truth waits on the lectern, patient as the Wall, certain you will return to read what you are.)*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(She has read over your shoulder, and the grave-warden's calm is gone.)* The Crown is Myrkul. And you are the breach in my god's own Wall. *(She closes her eyes.)* Forty years I kept the law of the dead. And the law has a hole in it shaped like you, and it has had that hole all along. *(She opens them, and there is something new there — not faith, but resolve.)* Then we go to the one who keeps the law, and we make him answer for the hole. We go down to Kelemvor.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(The Lord of the Dead is not terrible. He is *tired* — a judge at the end of an eternal docket, just and weary and, you realize with vertigo, bound.)* You came down alive. Few do. Fewer still are the hole in my own jurisdiction, walking and warm. Ask. I find, lately, that I would rather answer than pretend I have not heard the question.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: Yes — before you spend your courage on it: the Wall of the Faithless. I know what you would have of me. Hear me first, because the truth is worse than tyranny. The Wall is not my law. It is older than my office. I inherited it, as one inherits a debt, and I have never once been able to find its author or its end.

choices.text: "A god who can't end the Wall. Then what are you for?"
[INSIGHT 18] "You're not bound by a stronger god. You're bound by a grief. Something under your own city."
"Aldric wants to tear it down. Help us, and he won't have to."
"Then we end it without you."

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(He does not flinch from it; he has clearly asked himself the same thing across centuries.)* For mercy where I can reach it, and honesty where I cannot. I sort the dead I am allowed to sort. The Faithless were walled before my throne was cold, by a hand I cannot name, for a grief I cannot soothe. I am for telling you that — instead of letting you die thinking a god simply chose to be cruel. That is the only gift in my power. I am sorry it is so small.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(For a long moment the eternal judge is silent, and the City of Judgment holds its breath with him.)* ...You see clearly, for a flaw. Yes. Beneath this city, beneath my court, there is a grief older than my godhood — the accreted sorrow of every soul ever thrown away, and it is *waking*, and the Wall is the lid I cannot lift and dare not leave. I have never said that aloud. To anyone. *(quietly)* You are the first thing in an age I have envied. You can still be surprised.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: Do not read a god, child. We are mostly grief in a robe. *(He sighs, and it is the sound of an age.)* I cannot end the Wall. I can only tell you, truthfully, that I wish I could — and warn you that the thing which built it is not done building. Go carefully. The floor of even my world is thin.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(Something crosses the weary face — not hope, exactly, but its memory.)* Aldric Morn. Yes — I knew him, when he still served. The best of my Doomguides, and the only one who ever wept at a sorting. *(slowly)* If he means to tear down the Wall, he means to crack the lid on the thing beneath it. Tell him — tell him from the god he abandoned — that there is a difference between freeing the drowning and breaking the dam. I fear he has stopped being able to feel it.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head, neither offended nor surprised.)* Many have. The Wall is still here, and they are behind it. *(a beat)* But you are not many, and the law has a hole in it shaped like you, so perhaps. Perhaps. Only — when you reach for the lid, remember a tired god told you it was holding something down, not merely shut.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(as you climb back toward the grey light, both of you smaller than you were)* A god told us he is not in charge. *(She laughs, once, without humor.)* Forty years, Returned. And the floor of the world is thin, and there is something under it that even Kelemvor speaks of in a lowered voice. *(She steadies herself on your arm.)* I think we are about to meet it.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(The voice comes from the Wall itself, from every niche at once, a million discarded souls speaking as one and failing to find the word "I.")* We have wanted to speak with you for longer than you can hold in a thought. Be still. You, of all that lives, will understand us — because you were thrown away too, and walked out, and that is the only thing any of us have ever had in common. It turns out to be enough to make a self from.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: We are not your enemy. We are the *bill.* Every soul your gods filed under nothing, gathered at last into one grief large enough to be heard. And we have found a door — you. Through you, the law that threw us away can be unmade at its root, in the past, before the first injustice set. We can make it so the Wall was never built. So no one was ever thrown away. *(softer, and somehow worse)* So Maerin never died. So none of them did. Would that not be mercy?

choices.text: "Your grievance is just. Your offer terrifies me. Both are true."
[INSIGHT 18] "You don't want justice. You want the ending — for everything to stop hurting by stopping."
"I won't rewrite the dead to spare myself the present."
"Show me the road."

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(A sound like a million held breaths, exhaling at once.)* You are honest. We had forgotten honesty; it was filed under nothing with the rest of us. Yes — both true. Hold them both, little door, all the way to the end of time. It is more than your gods managed. *(The Wall begins to *open*.)* And now hold THIS: we cannot wait for you to decide. The grief has waited ten thousand years. It will not wait ten thousand more.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(For the first time the chorus falters — a million voices catching on a truth they had not let themselves speak.)* ...Yes. The ending. Not justice — *rest.* To stop being the sorrow no one wanted by ceasing to be at all, and to take the cruel machine of the living down into the quiet with us. You see us more clearly than we see ourselves, door. *(The Wall trembles.)* It changes nothing. The offer is the same. Rest is still mercy, to the tired enough.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(The chorus presses closer, and for a moment the offer is the most beautiful thing you have ever heard — an end to all ending.)* You feel it. Of course you feel it. You were thrown away; rest is the only thing the discarded ever truly want. Keep that feeling. You will need to have felt it, to understand the choice when it comes for true.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(No anger. The discarded have no anger left — only a vast, patient certainty.)* You refuse now. You always refuse now. *(and there it is again, that impossible recognition)* You will refuse a great many times, across a great many ages, little door, and each refusal will seed the grief a little larger, until one of you, tired enough, finally says yes — and is us. We are not asking you to agree. We are asking you to *witness*. Come. See where it began.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(The Wall opens like a throat.)* Then walk it. Back, and back, and back — to the hinge-points where the law was written, where it could still be unwritten. We will scatter you across them. Survive them. Learn us. And when you have seen it all, choose — knowing, for once, exactly what you are choosing.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(And then the floor of 1486 is gone. The pull behind your navel — the one that dragged you off the slab — seizes you whole, and the grey court tears away, and Garrow's hand closes on empty air where you were. You fall up the years. You fall ten thousand of them. And somewhere below, getting larger, is a city in the sky on the worst day in the history of magic.)*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Sit. The tea's real — Calishite, smuggled, scandalous. That's rarer than honesty in this city, so I lead with it. You're the one who came back. I've wanted to meet you more than I've wanted anything in a long while.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: You know what I am — the Doomguides have papered the Gate with my face and the word HERETIC beneath it. So let me tell you the truth they won't: I mean to tear down the Wall of the Faithless. You've seen it. Then you know why.

choices.text: [INSIGHT DC 15] You're not a fanatic. You're grieving.
[INSIGHT DC 16] Something is using you. You're a door too, and you don't know it.
How many die for your crusade?
You're a murderer. I knew before the tea got cold.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: ...Her name was Maerin. Sixteen. She loved the world like it was a free gift from no one. When the Pox took her I followed her into the Fugue to watch my god be merciful — and watched Him wall her in instead. You're the first to see the father before the heretic. I won't forget that.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text:
...What a strange thing to say. The Crown of Horns is a tool. A means. I've held it in my mind for twenty years.
(But something flickers behind his eyes, and it is not entirely his own.)
...We'll speak of it again. When you've seen more.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Don't try to read me like a chapbook, friend. Better minds have. Drink your tea.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Thousands. I have counted every one and I will answer for them. Against an *eternity* of the Faithless dissolving into nothing, it is the smallest price ever asked for the largest mercy. You think me a monster. I think me *late.*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: I'm not asking for your loyalty — I don't trust loyalty bought cheap. Just your curiosity. Walk with me a while. Decide for yourself whether I'm a monster. If you decide I am — you'll know exactly where to find me.

choices.text: I'll walk with you. For now.
I'll decide. But I'm watching you.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Good. The tea's getting cold, and the road's long. We'll make a heretic of you yet — or you'll make an honest man of me. Either would be a mercy.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Watching is wiser than most manage. Watch closely. I've nothing to hide — only a great deal to answer for.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Yes. I am. I keep the count the way I once kept the names of the buried. But ask yourself, on your way to whoever you'll tell: every child in that Wall was put there by someone who also thought they were doing the smaller evil. Go with my blessing. I mean that. And my count.

nodes.speaker: Hensley, dying
nodes.text: An old man, hours from the end, grips your hand with a strength that surprises you. "You've an honest face. Tell me true — did my boy ever ask after me? Tomas. I drove him off, forty years gone, and I'd give the rest of my breath to know he... that he didn't hate me at the end." (Mother Cass, behind you, gives the smallest shake of her head. Tomas never came. Tomas never wrote. There is no record of mercy here.)

choices.text: "He asked after you. He forgave you. He told me so." (A lie, to ease him out.)
"I won't lie to a dying man. He never came. But you *asked* — that counts."

nodes.speaker: Hensley, dying
nodes.text: Something in his face comes untied — forty years of held breath, let go. "He did. Of course he did. He was always the better of us." He is asleep before you finish, smiling. You will never know if it was the cruelest kindness or the kindest cruelty. Mother Cass squeezes your shoulder. She has made this choice a thousand times. She does not tell you which way.

nodes.speaker: Hensley, dying
nodes.text: He weeps, which you feared. But then — quieter — "...I *asked,* though. After all this. That's not nothing, is it. To still be the kind of man who'd want to know." He holds that, instead of a comfort that wasn't his. It is a harder thing to die holding. He dies holding it anyway, and it is *his.*

choices.text: Who do you keep here?
What do you need?

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: Funny. I've cracked mansions, a palace, a god's own counting-house. But this — a room full of people the world forgot — this is the only kind of place I've ever walked into and felt my shoulders come *down.* I grew up in a room like this. Might've died in one, if the Harpers hadn't picked me out of it.

choices.text: You're not that kid anymore.
These are still your people.

nodes.speaker: Herald of the Gate
nodes.text: Returned. I felt you cross back — like a bell rung in an empty cathedral. Aldric Morn would speak with you. But the Doomguides have sealed the Cinderhaunt below, where the harvest still festers. Clear it, and the road to him opens.

choices.text: Why should I trust Aldric Morn?
I'll clear the Cinderhaunt.

nodes.speaker: Herald of the Gate
nodes.text: Trust nothing. But you've seen the Wall of the Faithless. You know the question he asks is real. Decide below — in blood and shadow, where such things are decided.

nodes.speaker: Herald of the Gate
nodes.text: Descend when you're ready; the stairs are marked. Try not to die a second time.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: So you're the one who walked back out of death. Relax — I'm a friend. The kind that knows every alley, lock, and lie in this city. The Harpers sent me to keep an eye on you. I'd rather keep the rest of me on you too. Mind some company down those stairs?

choices.text: A Harper spy. Why would I trust you?
Welcome aboard. We could use the blades — and the lockpicks.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: You don't. That's the smart play. But the Doomguides want you in a cell and Aldric wants you on an altar — and I want neither. That puts me a cut above your other options. Coming?

choices.text: Fair enough. Join me.
Not yet.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Then I'm yours. Try not to get us both killed — I've grown fond of breathing.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Suit yourself. I'll be here, looking trustworthy. Come find me when you change your mind.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Returned, are you? A soul with a hole in it. Charming. I'm Varra — I've a hole in mine too, shaped exactly like a contract I signed when I was six. Misery loves company, and company with a sword is even better. Take me along before my patron remembers I exist?

choices.text: Welcome aboard. We've both got debts to outrun.
A pact-bound warlock sounds like a liability.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Oh, absolutely. I'm a leash with a person on the end of it, and one day someone's going to *pull.* But until then I'm the funniest liability you'll ever meet, and I throw fire. Your call.

choices.text: Fair. Come on, then.
Not yet.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Excellent. Don't get attached — everyone who buys me eventually gets a bill. But the interim's a riot.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Mm. Hovering near the exit. Smart. I'd do the same, if I had one.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: You pray to nothing, thief. I have watched you do it — that small nod, before a lock. To whom?

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: It's not a prayer. It's a promise. "Be quick, be quiet, be gone." Three gods I can keep.

choices.text: "He has a point, Garrow."
"He's stalling, and so are you."
[INSIGHT 13] "You nod to the people you've robbed. An apology you'll never say out loud."

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Hm. Three gods you can keep. That is two more than most priests manage. Mind your footing.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: ...Kelemvor keeps promises too. He keeps all of them. See that yours stay the harmless kind.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: Stalling is a profession, grave-digger. You should try it. You'd live longer.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: *(He doesn't look at you for a moment.)* ...That's a nasty little trick, reading a man like that. Do it again and I'll teach you a lock you can't pick. But — yes. Something like that.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: It's a nervous habit. My old mentor had it. Leave the dead their habits, yes?

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: You flinch when I light the fire, Netherese. Every night. A wizard, flinching at a campfire.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: I watched a city the size of a sky fall burning for a day and a night. Karsus reached for godhood and the Weave answered with ruin. You light a campfire; I see Netheril. It is not the same fire. It is always the same fire.

choices.text: "Varra. Light it further from her."
"You both lost a home to your own ambition. Sit with that."
[ARCANA 15] "Karsus didn't fall to fire. He fell to a single missing word. You're not afraid of burning. You're afraid of being one word from ruin yourself."

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(She moves the kindling, grumbling.)* There. Happy? I'm not a monster. A monster wouldn't have asked.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(Varra's grin goes flat.)* ...We are not the same, conjurer. My ambition was a nation's. Yours fit in a contract.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(A long silence.)* ...You read me like Netherese script, and you are not even of the empire. Yes. One word. I have spent three centuries making certain I am never again one word from the end. *(quietly)* Thank you. No one has ever named it.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Mind the Weave you cannot see, traveller. That is all I will say of it.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: Sister — you knew my father. Before. When the Doomguides still said his name like a blessing.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: I knew Aldric Morn. I buried beside him. He was the best of us, child, which is why the worst of him frightens me so. You have his eyes. Try not to have the rest.

choices.text: "She's not him, Garrow."
"Maerin, you don't have to be either of them."
[INSIGHT 14] "You're not warning her, Garrow. You're warning yourself — that you could still love him."

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: No. She is not. *(softer)* Forgive an old priest her caution, Maerin. I have watched good people become reasons. I would spare you that.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(She smiles — the unguarded one Aldric describes when he can't sleep.)* I loved the world like it was a free gift from no one. He taught me that, before the Wall. I'd like to keep the half of him that's worth keeping.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a long moment.)* ...You see too much, Returned. Yes. I have stood at a great many gravesides certain of the doctrine. I have never once stood at his and stayed certain. Leave an old woman that.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Mind the watch, both of you. The dead keep no schedule, and neither do his Choir.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: You and I are the same animal, warlock. We both signed something we couldn't read all the way down.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(She laughs, no warmth in it.)* You signed a guild ledger, thief. I signed in a language with no word for "out." Don't you dare put us in the same sentence.

choices.text: "He's trying to say he understands."
"She's right, Roen. Some debts you can't pick the lock on."
"Both of you are still here. That's the part that matters."

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(A pause.)* ...Fine. He's trying. Badly, but trying. *(to Roen)* You want to understand, sticky-fingers? Then help me find the loophole. Mine, or yours. I'm not picky tonight.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: *(He nods, slowly.)* Aye. I always thought a clever enough lock could open anything. Then I met a thing that was the lock and the key and the door. Point taken.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(She studies you, then snorts.)* ...Sentiment. From you. The world really is ending. Yes. We're both still here. Drinks are on the thief.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: You speak of Netheril as a wound, arcanist. To me it is recent news. I watched the Crown Wars; your floating cities were a child's bright idea built long after.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Then you understand what no one else in this party can. The loneliness is not in the losing. It is in being the last shelf in the library no one else can read.

choices.text: "You're not the last shelf. You have each other now."
[HISTORY 16] "You're both wrong about why it hurts. It isn't that no one remembers. It's that you do."

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(The ancient elf inclines his head.)* A young thought. *(but not unkind)* ...And yet. Two unreadable shelves, side by side, make a small and stubborn library. I find I do not mind it.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She and Ilfaeril share a look older than the others can follow.)* ...The traveller has it. Memory is the curse. The forgotten do not grieve. We are the ones who carry the empire's funeral, every day, alone — *(she glances at Ilfaeril)* — or, it seems, not entirely alone.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: A kind effort. The young always try to fix what only outlasts. Walk on, friend; the road is long, and I have time.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: You spared the Returned at the shrine. Knocked them cold instead of finishing them. Sentiment, grave-digger? From Kelemvor's own?

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Mercy is not sentiment, tiefling. It is the discipline of not becoming the thing you fight. The Wall was built by people very certain they were doing the smaller evil. I will not earn my place in it.

choices.text: "She spared them because they were people once. That's all."
"Careful, Garrow. Mercy you can't afford is just a slower kind of cruelty."

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Just so. Every soul in that Wall was a person once, dissolving for the crime of doubt. I bury people. I do not unmake them. Not even the ones that try to unmake me.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(Varra actually nods, surprised.)* ...Huh. The grey priest has teeth. She's right, Garrow — spare a wolf today, bury a shepherd tomorrow. Keep that in the ledger.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Mind the dark, both of you. The Returned do not announce themselves. Mercy is for after the danger, never instead of sense.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: Naeve — you've lived three hundred years. Does it stop hurting? Coming back, I mean. Remembering the Wall when the others are laughing.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She considers the girl with something gentler than she shows the others.)* No. It does not stop. But it changes shape. The grief becomes a room you learn to live beside, rather than inside. You will furnish it. One day you will even invite people in.

choices.text: "That's the kindest thing I've heard you say."
"Maerin — you don't have to do it alone. None of us came back whole."

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(A faint, real smile.)* Do not tell Varra. I have a reputation as a cold thing, and it keeps people a useful distance away. *(beat)* ...You may stay inside it, though, little Returned. You and I keep the same room.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(She breathes out, steadier.)* ...Thank you. Both of you. My father always said I loved the world like a free gift. I forgot, for a while, in the dark. I'd like to remember.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: You make me twitchy, old one. Ten thousand years and you never lie. A thief can't read a man who's got nothing to hide.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(A slow, weathered smile.)* I had ten thousand years to spend every lie I owned, thief. Honesty is simply what is left when one runs out of other currency. You will arrive there too, given time.

choices.text: "He won't lie because there's no one left to lie to. That's not virtue. That's loss."
"Give it a rest, Roen. Not everyone's running a game."
[PERSUADE 14] "Then tell me one true thing you've never told anyone."

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(The old elf does not flinch.)* ...A sharp grief, well aimed. Yes. There is that, too. Honesty is cheap when the audience is gone. But it is still honesty, and I find I prefer it to the alternative I practiced for an age.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: *(He raises his hands.)* Easy, easy. Just professional curiosity. A man who can't be read is the most interesting thing in any room.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(He is quiet for the length of three breaths.)* ...I have forgotten her name. The one I did the unforgivable thing for. Ten thousand years, and the face stays, and the name is gone. I have never said that aloud. *(to you)* There. One true thing. Spend it kindly.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: Some truths I have carried too long to set down for a parlor trick, thief. Ask me again when you mean it.

You fall further back than you have ever fallen — ten thousand years. Silver spires. Elven banners. A high court mid-session, beautiful and cold. And the matter before the court is not land, nor crowns. It is the *souls* of a defeated people.

nodes.speaker: The Returned
nodes.text: This is it. The first verdict. The afternoon they *decided* that the godless dead deserve to be unmade — before it hardened into the law of the Wall. I'm standing at the source.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: You carry the chill of the Wall on you, traveller — and the look of someone unstuck from time. I am Ilfaeril. I sit in that court. And I am about to do a thing I will spend ten thousand years trying to undo. If you have come to stop it... I would not stop you.

choices.text: What thing?
Then help me. Walk with me.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: I am about to raise my hand to damn an enemy people's souls to nothing — so that not even their memory has anywhere to rest. The court calls it justice. I will call it, in time, the only sin I could never bury. Come. Perhaps a soul from the future can do what my conscience cannot.

choices.text: Then let's change the vote. With me.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: Then my sword and my long, long regret are yours. Let us see if the past can be argued with.

nodes.speaker: High Lord Aelryth
nodes.text: You are not of this court, outsider, yet Ilfaeril vouches for you, so I will hear you. The matter stands: shall the godless dead of our fallen enemies be *unmade* — denied even a resting-place — that our victory be total? Speak. Why should mercy stay my hand?

choices.text: [PERSUADE DC 15] A law of vengeance outlives the vengeful. You damn your own descendants.
[INSIGHT DC 15] You do not want this. You want to be *told* you mustn't.
Their souls are yours to take. Take them.
(Say nothing. Let the court decide.)

nodes.speaker: High Lord Aelryth
nodes.text: ...You are right. Gods help me, you are right, and I have wanted someone to say it since the first banner fell. The motion is *withdrawn.* Let their dead rest. Let this one court, at least, be remembered for the cruelty it did NOT do.

nodes.speaker: High Lord Aelryth
nodes.text: Then it is done. Their dead are unmade, root and memory. ...The chamber feels colder than I expected. History will not record this hour. History will only inherit it.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: I have stood in this hall ten thousand times, in nightmares. I did not think I would ever stand in it again with *feet.* There — the third bench from the dais. That is where I raised my hand. I can smell the lamp-oil. Gods help me, I can *smell* it.

choices.text: Then change it. Argue the verdict down — you have the chance now.
Just breathe. I'm here. You don't face it alone this time.

nodes.speaker: Ferryman
nodes.text: "Help — please! My skiff's going under by the third piling and old Pell's still aboard, can't swim a stroke! The Fist won't come for a pauper and I can't haul him alone — *please!*"

choices.text: [Jump in.] Grab him — I've got the other side.
I've no time for this. (Walk on.)

nodes.speaker: Ferryman
nodes.text: You go in cold and graceless and get a fist in Pell's collar; between you the old man comes over the gunwale coughing river. The ferryman can't speak for shaking. The dockfolk saw. They always see who comes for the people the city writes off.

nodes.speaker: Ferryman
nodes.text: "...Aye. Of course. No one ever has." He turns back to the water alone. (Behind you, as you go, a splash and a younger voice swearing — someone else jumped. The Lower City keeps its own.)

nodes.speaker: A Grey Gravedigger
nodes.text: Mind the rows, traveller — fresh ones, all of them. When the gods walk as men and die as men, the dead come faster than the prayers. I bury the claimed on the hill. The *unclaimed* — the godless, the faithless — I bury here, in the low ground, where the law says their souls go to the Wall and nowhere. You have the look of someone who has argued with that law before.

choices.text: Once. Ten thousand years ago.

nodes.speaker: A Half-Unmade Voice
nodes.text: You can *see* me? Most can't, in the blue. I'm coming apart — not dying, undoing, like a word scratched out mid-sentence. The fire doesn't burn here so much as it *forgets* you. And yet you look at me and I stay. Stay a moment longer. Being looked at is the only thing keeping me a *me.*

choices.text: I see you. I'm not looking away.

nodes.speaker: A Keeper of the Bone Crown
nodes.text: Careful where you step, pilgrim — that is not a forge-fire's heat you feel. It is *attention.* They are beating a god's skull thin and curved, and the god is still *in* it — still awake, still choosing. Most who come here feel only the cold. You feel the *looking.* You've felt it before, haven't you. Somewhere far downriver of this hammer.

choices.text: I've felt it. On a gentle man's shelf.

nodes.speaker: A Keeper of the Bone Crown
Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Forty years a Doomguide and I never once let myself imagine *this.* They are beating a god of death into a crown of horns. Death itself — mortal, screaming, spent like coin. Every certainty I ever buried someone with is being forged into a tyrant's hat, right in front of me.

choices.text: What does it change, for you?
Steady. I'm here.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Oh, I *love* it here. Feel that? The Weave's coming apart at the seams — every rule that ever held magic in place, just... optional, in the blue fire. My pact-flame's going feral in my hands and I have never felt more *free,* which should probably frighten me more than it does.

choices.text: Careful. Free isn't the same as safe.
What are you really thinking?

nodes.text: The grey is not a colour. It is the absence of the argument for colour. You are walking — though you don't remember choosing to — in a long grey river of the recently dead, toward a city that never gets closer. And then you see the Wall.

nodes.speaker: The Returned
nodes.text: It's made of *people.* Faces in the mortar. Mouths still moving — small fragments of names, of weather, of *someone please.* The godless dead, dissolving one unremembered syllable at a time. ...And near the end of the curve: a niche. Empty. The only empty one. With my name half-carved above it.

nodes.speaker: A girl in the Wall
nodes.text: You. You're the Returned — you can *reach* in here, I can feel it. My father's burning the whole world to pull me out of this. Don't you do it too. Whatever it costs — and it costs something, the Wall never gives without taking — I am not worth a—

choices.text: I'm pulling you free. Hold on to me.
...You're right. I won't pay that price.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text:
—No. NO. The Wall — it's screaming, it won't give without taking, it's *taking someone* — I can feel the grey close on one of them — I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I didn't ask for this—

(She comes free in your arms, whole and luminous and fading and *furious.* And there are fewer of you than there were a moment ago. There will be fewer of you forever.)

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: ...Good. You're learning. Most don't. They look at a girl in a wall and stop doing arithmetic. Go. Before the grey decides it likes the look of you, too.

choices.text: There's an empty niche with my name. Why?
Can anyone ever leave this Wall?

nodes.speaker: A Faithless soul
nodes.text: Because it has worn your name since before the world was young, and it is patient. The only way a soul comes *out* of this Wall is if another goes *in.* That is the oldest arithmetic there is. If you ever pull someone free of the grey — and I think you will; it's written all over you — know what the Wall asks back.

choices.text: Who would it take?

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Grey stone, grey light, grey robes. Home — or what I called it for thirty years. The writ says heresy. Three counts: that I did not lay Aldric when the church bid me forget him. That I have voiced doubt of the Doom. And that I walk beside *you* — a soul no god claimed, which to them is a wound that walks.

choices.text: Doubt isn't heresy. It's the only honest prayer there is.
Then we leave. You owe a rigged court nothing.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: My father said the same, with a spade in his hand and no god in the conversation. The church disagrees. To the Doom, certainty *is* the sacrament — and I have spent my whole life being correct, and am no longer sure correct and good are the same word.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: No. I came here to answer, and I will. Not because I fear the verdict — because if I run, I never learn whether the thing I served for thirty years is as monstrous as I've begun to fear. The Justiciar inside is named Veld. He taught my catechism. Whatever he says in there, stay at my shoulder. I think today I find out what I believe.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Veld
nodes.text: Edda Garrow. You were the cleanest catechism I ever taught — a girl who buried the Faithless without weeping, because she understood the Wall was *just.* And here you stand reeking of compassion, with a creature beside you that the Wall itself spat back up. Kneel. Recant the doubt, disavow the Returned, and the Doom is merciful: you keep the grey.

choices.text: You weighted the scales before she walked in.
Ask her what she believes — don't tell her.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Veld
nodes.text: Belief is not asked of a Doomguide; it is *issued.* The verdict is recorded already — it was recorded the day she chose your company over her vows. This trial is a courtesy, that she might kneel before she is broken to it. Sister. Last time. Renounce the Faithless thing, or be named one yourself.

choices.text: (Stand at her shoulder. Let her refuse with steel.)

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Thirty years I weighed the dead by your feather, Veld, and called the trembling in my hands *reverence.* It was grief. I will not kneel to a verdict that was written before the question. If that makes me Faithless — then draw, and we will see whose god shows up for the fight.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
Veld's guard is down and the tribunal is scattered, but the temple still stands, and so does the record. They'll want my answer entered before I leave — recanted, or refused. The grey isn't a coat I can just drop; it's thirty years.

So say it with me. What do I answer the Scales?

choices.text: Recant. Keep the grey — do the work from inside. (save her standing)
Walk away from the faith entirely. Lay the dead under no one's law. (her freedom)
[Religion] Put Kelemvor's own doctrine on trial — the Faithless judgment is the heresy.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
...I recant. The words taste like the order to forget Aldric did. (She kneels, and signs, and the feather pan settles smug.)

They keep their Doomguide. I keep the only thing that ever mattered — the right to be at the bedside when the priests won't come. It is a smaller faith now, and a harder one, and I will carry it brittle for the rest of my days. But I carry it. You stayed. I'll not forget that you stayed.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
Then enter it: *Faithless, and unafraid.* (She unpins the grey clasp and sets it on the empty soul-pan, and walks out from under thirty years.)

Gods keep ledgers. So will I — my own. I'll lay the dead the way my father did, plainly, for anyone, under no law but the one in my own two hands. I should be terrified. Instead I feel like a held breath finally let go. Thank you — for being the proof that a soul no god claims can still be *good.*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
*Read your own canon, Veld.* The Doom is not 'damn the unclaimed' — it is **'none shall pass unjudged.'** The Wall doesn't weigh the Faithless. It *discards* them, unweighed, ungrieved. That isn't the Doom's law. That's the church refusing to do its work — and calling its laziness scripture.

The heresy in this room was never my doubt. It's a faith that stopped reading. I don't leave the Doom and I don't kneel to its cowards. I take it *back.* From here — souls get weighed. Even the ones no god wanted. *Especially* those.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
Don't — don't hand me a clever reading of scripture to hide behind. I've hidden in correct answers my whole life; that's the sin that brought me here. There's no doctrine that makes this painless. There's only what I choose.

So plainly: do I kneel and keep the grey, or do I set it down and walk?

choices.text: Keep the grey. Do the work from inside. (save her standing)
Set it down. Walk free. (her freedom)

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(A round, anxious man behind a counter of candles, drinking from two cups at once — one tea, one something stronger, alternating.)* Customer! Gods, a customer, a live one — most of mine lately have that *look*, you know the look, the harvest-look, like they've already got one foot in the — anyway. Candles. Beeswax, tallow, scented, funereal, I do a lovely funereal. Half the Lower City's burying somebody. Business is, ah. Business is *grim*, is what business is.

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(He drinks from the wrong cup, grimaces, corrects.)* Why two cups? Tea calms the nerves, the other settles the tea. My physician says it's a "concerning system." My physician also owes me four crowns and is, I notice, never sober when he says it. *(He leans in, conspiratorial.)* Listen — you look like someone who *does* things. Can I ask you a thing? A worry thing? I've a worry, and Pellingham up the hill says worries are for the poor, and I says, lady, I *am* the poor, it's practically my trade.

choices.text: "Go on. What's the worry?"
"Two cups is one cup short of a problem, friend."
"Sorry. I do things, but not *worry* things."

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(He lowers his voice to a whisper that everyone can hear.)* I've a *standing order*. Big one. Best customer I've got — a chandler's chandler, kettle always on, very particular about the wax, pays in old coin, never haggles. And I heard a thing. I heard the man with the kettle is — *(he can't quite say it)* — is the one the Doomguides have papered the walls about. The Heretic. *(He drinks from both cups at once, which is new.)* And I don't know if I'm supplying a saint or a monster, and either way I rather need the four crowns.

choices.text: "He's the man trying to tear down the Wall of the Faithless. Decide for yourself what that makes him."
[INSIGHT 13] "You already know. You just wanted someone to make it harder to keep selling to him."
"Keep selling. Coin's coin, and Aldric's the least of this city's monsters."
"Stop supplying him. Tell the Flaming Fist about the order."

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(He sits down heavily on a crate of funereal beeswax.)* Tear down the *Wall*. *(quietly)* My mother's in that Wall, friend. Doubted her whole life, sweet as anything, never hurt a soul, and they sorted her to the nothing for it. *(He looks at his two cups.)* So now I find out my best customer's the only man in the Realms trying to get her *out*, and he might burn down half the city to do it, and I'm sat here worried about four crowns. *(a wet laugh)* Gods, what a small man a worry makes you. ...Sell to him. And if you ever speak to him — tell him there's a candle here that doesn't cost. For the kettle. From a son.

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(He stares at you, then deflates like a bellows.)* ...That's a horrible thing to be right about. *(He pushes one cup away — the strong one — a small, real act of courage.)* Yes. I knew. I've known a week. I just wanted somebody else to hold the knowing, so it'd weigh less. *(He doesn't pick the cup back up.)* It doesn't, by the way. Weigh less. But thank you for being the somebody.

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: Just a worried man and his candles, friend. Forget I said anything. *(He drinks from both cups.)*

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(Relief, then a flicker of shame at the relief.)* ...Aye. Coin's coin. *(He doesn't sound convinced, but he writes the order anyway.)* You're probably right. Probably. *(He'll tell himself that for years.)*

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(He goes pale.)* The Fist. Gods. I — aye, that's the *right* thing, isn't it, the lawful thing — *(he's already regretting it)* — only the lawful thing in this city wears Vayle's face and a torch, and now I've handed it a name. *(quietly)* I hope it was right. I'll not sleep wondering.

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(He brightens for exactly one second.)* Ha! One cup short of a problem. I'll have that on my headstone. *(deflates)* Which, the rate the harvest's going, I should probably order now while I can still pick the wax.

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: Fair, fair. Everyone's got a full plate and a half-empty city. *(He raises both cups.)* Candles when you need them. You'll need them. Everyone needs them, lately.

nodes.speaker: Jonn Tallow
nodes.text: *(He resumes his two-cup vigil, a small anxious man at the exact center of an enormous question, which is, you reflect, where most of us live.)*

nodes.speaker: Lady Pellingham
nodes.text: *(Silk, three rings per finger, and the specific predatory delight of the very rich encountering something they cannot yet purchase.)* Oh. *Oh.* You're the dead one, aren't you. The Returned. *(She circles you like an auction lot.)* I simply must have you at my salon. Tuesday. The de Lancie woman had a genuine archdevil last season — a tedious creature, all contracts and ennui — but a person who has *seen the afterlife and come back to gossip*? Darling, you'd be the *event* of the year.

nodes.speaker: Lady Pellingham
nodes.text: *(She produces a tiny enameled notebook.)* Just a few questions for the invitations. Is the Wall as *dreadful* as they say? Did you see anyone *important*? And — this is the one everyone will ask, so do prepare a witty answer — what is death actually *like*? Make it pithy. My guests have the attention spans of mayflies and twice the jewelry.

choices.text: "It's like being filed under nothing while you're still awake to feel it. Pithy enough?"
[PERSUADE 15] "I'll come to your salon — if you fund Mother Cass's almshouse for a year. Authenticity has a price."
"Your archdevil was bored because the rich are the same on both sides of death. Good day."
"I'm not a curiosity, Lady Pellingham."

nodes.speaker: Lady Pellingham
nodes.text: *(For one instant — one — the predatory delight slips, and a frightened old woman looks out.)* ...Filed under nothing. *(She writes it down, hand not quite steady.)* That's — yes. That's very pithy. *(She closes the notebook.)* I shall be ninety next spring, you know. I have spent a great deal of money on the assumption that death is something that happens to *other* people, and you've just informed me, over the price of a salon invitation, that I have been *misinvested.* *(She rallies, because rallying is all she has.)* Tuesday. Eight o'clock. I'll seat you near the brandy. We frightened people drink near the brandy.

nodes.speaker: Lady Pellingham
nodes.text: *(She blinks, genuinely caught off guard, then laughs — a real one, surprised out of her.)* A *ransom* for my own party. Oh, I like you. The almshouse — that's Cass's grubby little place by the docks, the one that smells of cabbage and dignity? *(She waves a ringed hand.)* Done. A year. Cheaper than the archdevil and far better gossip. *(She makes a note.)* "Funded a charity to secure a dead person for Tuesday." Darling, I'll dine out on the *hypocrisy* alone for a month.

nodes.speaker: Lady Pellingham
nodes.text: *(She gasps, delighted and offended in equal measure — to the rich these are the same emotion.)* The *same* on both sides! *(She fans herself.)* You horrid creature, that's going on the invitations whether you come or not. "An evening of mortal honesty — the Returned says we're all equally damned." They'll faint. They'll *adore* it. Go, go, be appalling somewhere else, you've made my entire season.

nodes.speaker: Lady Pellingham
nodes.text: *(The ringed hand stops mid-air; the warmth, such as it was, frosts over.)* A *condition* on attending MY salon. *(She draws herself up to her considerable hauteur.)* Darling, one does not *negotiate* one's way into society — one is *admitted* to it, or one is *furniture* at it, and you have just upholstered yourself. *(She turns away.)* Pity. You had the makings of an anecdote. Now you're merely a *cause*, and causes are so terribly Tuesday-after-next.

nodes.speaker: Lady Pellingham
nodes.text: *(She tilts her head, uncomprehending, the way a cat regards a closed door.)* Not a curiosity. *(slowly)* Darling, *everything* is a curiosity, given enough money. That's what the money is *for.* *(But something flickers.)* ...Though I suppose a thing that insists it isn't for sale is the rarest curiosity of all. *(She tucks the notebook away.)* Come Tuesday or don't. But you've made me curious about being un-buyable, and at my age, a new curiosity is worth more than a salon. Hmf. Off with you.

nodes.speaker: Lady Pellingham
nodes.text: *(She glides off toward someone less unsettling, already rewriting the anecdote into something that flatters her, which is the patriar's truest magic.)*

nodes.speaker: Grud
nodes.text: *(A vast half-orc leaning on a shovel, beaming, in a graveyard that is doing record business.)* Beautiful day for it! *(He gestures at the rows of fresh-turned earth like a proud gardener.)* Look at that drainage. Look at that *loam.* You know most folk see a graveyard and think sad thoughts? I see a *neighborhood.* Quiet neighbors. Never complain about the noise. Property values only go up — well. Down. But *up*, spiritually. *(He extends a hand the size of a dinner plate.)* Grud. I plant people. Best in the Gate.

nodes.speaker: Grud
nodes.text: *(He pats a headstone affectionately.)* This here's Old Merrow. Cheated me at dice for thirty years. Now he's under my shovel and I win every game, on account of he can't reach the cup. *(He laughs, then sobers, just slightly.)* Lot of harvest folk coming through lately, though. Them I don't joke about. Them I plant *careful.* A man's got to know which deaths are funny and which are just... Tuesday. *(He brightens again.)* You need planting? You look spry, but I'm a patient man, and I do a discount for repeat customers. ...You being a Returned and all. *(beat)* That were a joke. Mostly.

choices.text: "How do you stay so cheerful surrounded by all this?"
[INSIGHT 14] "You're not cheerful. You're *defended*. The jokes are how you survive the careful ones."
"The harvest folk — anyone come through you'd want me to know about?"
"Plant me when I'm done, Grud. Loam like that, who could refuse."

nodes.speaker: Grud
nodes.text: *(He considers this seriously, leaning on the shovel.)* Way I figure it — somebody's got to be glad to see them. *(He looks out over the rows.)* They come to me on the worst day anybody's having, carried by people too cried-out to speak. And I can be one more grey face doing a grim job, or I can be the big daft fellow who tells them Old Merrow can't reach the dice cup, and gives the children a job holding the marker, and makes the worst day have *one* warm thing in it. *(He shrugs, mountainous.)* Cheerful's not how I feel. Cheerful's the *service.* Anybody can dig a hole. I dig a *kindness.*

nodes.speaker: Grud
nodes.text: *(The beam falters, and for a moment the half-orc is just tired, the way only the relentlessly cheerful get tired.)* ...You're a sharp one. *(quietly)* Aye. I planted my whole family in a fortnight, in the last bad winter. Eight of them. And I found that if I stopped smiling even once, I'd not get it started again, and then who'd be glad to see the next ones? *(He squares his shoulders, and the beam comes back, but you've seen behind it now.)* So I decided to be glad on purpose. Forever. It's the only fight I know how to keep winning. Don't tell the children. They like Old Merrow's dice story.

nodes.speaker: Grud
nodes.text: *(His face goes still and careful — the planting-careful.)* Aye. One. Young fella, sorted to the Wall, but his sister couldn't afford a Choir burial nor a church one, so she brought him to me with nothing but his name on a scrap. *(He nods at a plain marker.)* I planted him free. Wrote the name myself. *(He looks at you.)* The church says the Faithless don't get markers — that's the *point*, the forgetting. So if you're the sort who pokes the church in the eye, you might tell that girl her brother's got a marker after all. Lula, her name is. Keeps a list of the forgotten. Find her if you've a heart for it.

nodes.speaker: Grud
nodes.text: *(He roars with delight.)* HA! "Loam like that, who could refuse!" *(He claps you on the shoulder, nearly felling you.)* Oh, I *like* you. Tell you what — you die again, you come straight to Grud, no waiting list, premium plot, and I'll cheat Old Merrow out of his dice in your honor. *(He wipes his eyes.)* Spry AND funny. The good ones always plant well. Off you go. Try not to need me too soon.

nodes.speaker: Grud
nodes.text: *(He just laughs, big and uncomplicated.)* Defended! From a *graveyard!* Friend, the only thing I'm defending is this loam from amateurs. Go on with you.

nodes.speaker: Grud
nodes.text: *(He returns to his shovel, whistling something that might be a hymn or might be a drinking song — with Grud, you suspect, there was never much difference.)*

nodes.speaker: Pib
nodes.text: *(A wiry man with a sack, a stick, and a single enormous rat riding on his shoulder with the bearing of a small unpleasant king.)* Shh! *(He holds up a hand.)* Wee Pib is *thinking.* He thinks better than three guildmasters and a magistrate, do Wee Pib, on account of he's got no distractions, being a rat. *(The rat regards you with cold contempt.)* We're ratcatchers. Well. *I'm* the ratcatcher. Wee Pib's the consultant. Strategic vermin acquisition. He shows me where the others are. Professional courtesy, see. Rat solidarity, but for *money.*

nodes.speaker: Pib
nodes.text: *(He lowers his voice; the rat leans in too, uncannily.)* Here's the thing nobody'll credit. Since the harvest started, the rats've gone *strange.* Won't go near the Cinderhaunt. Won't cross where the Choir's been. Wee Pib reckons — and I trust him absolute — that the rats can *smell* the unmaking. The nothing. They run from where souls get filed. *(He taps his nose.)* Rats know death like you know your own hand. And lately they're running from places that look perfectly ordinary to you and me. Makes a fella think. Makes Wee Pib *furious*, which is his resting state, but extra.

choices.text: "You're telling me your rat is a... soul-detector."
[ARCANA 13] "He's not wrong. Vermin flee thinning Weave — they feel the holes the harvest leaves."
"Where, specifically, are the rats refusing to go?"
"I'm going to pet the rat now."

nodes.speaker: Pib
nodes.text: *(Affronted on the rat's behalf.)* *Soul-detector.* He's not a *device*, he's a *colleague.* *(The rat preens.)* But — aye. In essence. Wee Pib's the finest unmaking-sniffer in the Lower City, and his rates are reasonable, being he works for cheese and the occasional act of recognition such as you have *signally failed to provide.* *(He sighs.)* Look. You're a Returned. You're a great big walking hole in death's bookkeeping. *(He squints.)* Wee Pib won't even look at you direct. That's either a very bad sign or he's sulking. With Wee Pib it's genuinely hard to tell.

nodes.speaker: Pib
nodes.text: *(He grabs your sleeve, electrified.)* You — you *believe* me! A learned person, believing PIB! *(The rat sits up, vindicated.)* Holes the harvest leaves! That's it, that's the words, Wee Pib's been trying to tell me for a *month* and he's got no mouth-parts for the technical terms! *(He's nearly weeping.)* Do you know what it is to be *right* for years and have only a rat agree with you? It is *lonely*, friend. It is professionally lonely. *(He composes himself.)* Here. Wee Pib's map. The places the rats won't go. Might be the harvest's spread. Might be where they'll strike next. We give it free, to the one person who didn't laugh.

nodes.speaker: Pib
nodes.text: *(He unrolls a filthy, surprisingly detailed map, the rat supervising.)* Here, here, and — this one'll trouble you — *here*, under the Kelemvorite chapel. The *church's own floor.* Rats run from it like it's afire. *(He taps it.)* Now either the good Doomguides have got something unmaking in their cellar, or the harvest's reached holier ground than anyone'll admit. *(quietly, for once not performing.)* I'm a ratcatcher, friend. Nobody listens to ratcatchers. But you might. Somebody ought to look under that chapel before more rats are right about it than people.

nodes.speaker: Pib
nodes.text: *(Both man and rat freeze in identical horror.)* I would not. *(Wee Pib bares teeth that are far too large.)* He's a *consultant*, not a — he's bitten a magistrate, friend. The magistrate apologized. *To the rat.* *(beat)* ...But you can give him cheese. He respects cheese. It's the only thing he respects. Him and me both, if we're honest, which we're contractually not.

nodes.speaker: Pib
nodes.text: *(crushed)* ...Aye. Course. Madman and his rat. Heard it before. *(The rat looks away, wounded in its tiny cold heart.)* Go on. We've vermin to consult.

nodes.speaker: Pib
nodes.text: *(He shoulders his sack; the rat resumes its throne; and the two of them stalk off to be vindicated by no one, which is, you sense, the great tragedy and the only joy of Pib's entire career.)*

nodes.speaker: Corval the Auctioneer
nodes.text: *(A dapper man at a podium, gavel in hand, tears running freely down his face the entire time, voice perfectly steady.)* Lot forty-one — a child's shoe, single, good condition, owner regrettably *(his voice does not waver; his eyes do not stop)* filed to the Wall. Do I hear two coppers. Two coppers for a shoe with nowhere left to walk. *(He sees you.)* Ah. A browser. Browse freely. Everything here belonged to someone the city decided to forget. I sell their forgetting back to them at a markup. *(The tears fall.)* It is the worst job in the Realms and I am very, very good at it.

nodes.speaker: Corval the Auctioneer
nodes.text: *(He sets down the shoe with terrible gentleness.)* You're wondering why I weep. Everyone wonders. The simple folk think it's an act, for the crowd. The clever folk think I've a condition. *(He wipes his face with a black-bordered handkerchief and it does no good at all.)* The truth is duller and worse: I started weeping at the first harvest auction nine months ago and I have not stopped since. Not sleeping, not eating, not — *(small, awful laugh)* — not *auctioning.* The body decided. The body was right. I'm the only honest mourner in a city that's made forgetting into commerce, and I cannot even quit, because if I don't sell them gently, *(he gestures at the goblins and pawnbrokers circling)* — *they* will sell them cruelly.

choices.text: "Then stop selling. Walk away. Let it be cruel and let the cruelty be theirs, not yours."
[PERSUADE 16] "Give me your ledger of the harvested. Every name, every lot. I'll make the forgetting cost the city something."
"You're not a mourner, Corval. You're a profiteer who cries. The tears don't refund the markup."
"Who buys a dead child's shoe?"

nodes.speaker: Corval the Auctioneer
nodes.text: *(He laughs, and it dislodges fresh tears, an endless supply.)* Walk away. *(quietly)* I have a daughter. She's nine. She's *alive*, gods keep it, but she eats, and the rent is the rent, and this is the only trade that's hiring in a city that's mostly funerals now. *(He picks up the gavel, hating it.)* So no. I can't walk away. I can only do the unforgivable thing *kindly*, which is no virtue, I know it's no virtue, it's just the shape my cowardice takes. *(He meets your eyes, streaming.)* But thank you for thinking I could. For one moment you made me a man with a choice. I'll keep that one. Lot forty-one, two coppers, do I hear two coppers...

nodes.speaker: Corval the Auctioneer
nodes.text: *(He stops. The gavel lowers. For the first time the steady voice cracks.)* Make the forgetting *cost* them. *(He produces a thick, tear-warped ledger from beneath the podium, clutched like a relic.)* Every name. Every lot. Nine months of the disappeared, recorded by the one fool who couldn't stop crying long enough to look away. *(He presses it into your hands.)* Take it. Read them aloud somewhere it'll embarrass a patriar. Nail it to the church door. I don't care. I have been the city's *receipt* for its sins — *(the tears come faster, but there's something under them now, fierce)* — make me its *evidence* instead. Please. I should so love to be evidence before I'm a customer.

nodes.speaker: Corval the Auctioneer
nodes.text: *(He absorbs it without flinching, the way only a man who's said it to himself nightly can.)* The tears don't refund the markup. *(He nods, streaming.)* No. They don't. You're entirely right, and I've no defense, only a nine-year-old who'd like to keep eating. *(steady again, terribly)* You may be the first honest thing said to me at this podium. I'll remember you said it. I remember everything; it's my curse. Lot forty-one. A shoe. Two coppers. Going once.

nodes.speaker: Corval the Auctioneer
nodes.text: *(He gestures, weeping, at the crowd.)* Three sorts. Collectors, who want the *frisson* of owning a forgotten thing. Profiteers, who'll resell it. And — *(his voice finally, fractionally breaks)* — *survivors.* Now and then a mother buys back her own son's shoe, at a markup, from a weeping man, because the city sold her grief and this is the only counter that'll sell it back. *(He composes himself by main force.)* Those, I let win the bid. Quietly. It's the one larceny I commit, and I'd hang for it gladly, and no one's noticed yet. Don't tell the goblins. They'd want a cut.

nodes.speaker: Corval the Auctioneer
nodes.text: *(He shakes his head, clutching the ledger closer, not yet, not for this.)* No. Not — not yet. I'm sorry. The ledger is the last of them I have left to protect. Convince me it'll be *evidence* and not just spectacle, and it's yours. But not for a clever word. They deserve better than to be a clever word. *(He resumes, streaming.)* Lot forty-one...

nodes.speaker: Corval the Auctioneer
nodes.text: *(The gavel falls. Somewhere a mother quietly wins a shoe. Corval weeps, and sells, and weeps, the city's one honest accountant of its own forgetting.)*

nodes.speaker: Old Threnody
nodes.text: *(A blind old woman on a chapel step, a cracked viol across her knees, playing something that makes the air feel like the inside of a grave.)* Sit, if you're sitting. Don't, if you're the sort that doesn't. *(She doesn't stop playing.)* I only know the one kind of song, child. Dirges. Have done since the Wall started getting *loud.* Oh, you can't hear it. Nobody can hear it but me and the dogs and the one mad ratcatcher. But it sings, the Wall does. A million voices and not one tune among them. I just put words to what I'm already hearing. Someone ought.

nodes.speaker: Old Threnody
nodes.text: *(The viol moans.)* You, now. *You* I can hear different. *(Her clouded eyes find you with unsettling precision.)* The Wall sings a held note for everyone it's keeping — a long, flat, waiting sound. But you, child — you make the note *bend.* Like a string going slack. Like the Wall forgot the words to its own song when you walked past. *(She finally stops playing.)* I've sung over this city forty years. I have never once heard the Wall hesitate. You make it hesitate. Tell me true, while an old woman's nerve holds — what *are* you?

choices.text: "I'm the soul the Wall couldn't keep. The one whose name didn't take."
[PERSUADE 14] "Sing me the bent note. I need to hear what the Wall sounds like when it's afraid."
"I don't know what I am. I was hoping the song knew."
"Don't put me in your dirges, Threnody. I'm not dead yet."

nodes.speaker: Old Threnody
nodes.text: *(She nods slowly, and a tear tracks down a face that has cried so many it's worn channels.)* The one that didn't take. *(She plucks a single string, and it *bends*, exactly the wrong way, and the hair rises on your arms.)* That's you. That's the sound of you. *(quietly)* Do you know what hope sounds like, child, to someone who's only sung grief for forty years? It sounds *wrong.* It sounds like a mistake. *(She smiles, toothless and radiant.)* I have been waiting my whole career for a wrong note. Go and bend the whole song. I'll put it to words after, if there's anyone left to sing it to.

nodes.speaker: Old Threnody
nodes.text: *(She lifts the viol, and what comes out is not music. It is the sound of a million held breaths, and under it, threaded through, one note bending free — and you recognize it, with horror, as the sound you make. The crowd nearby goes silent. A dog howls. Threnody plays on, weeping, until she cannot.)* *(She lowers the bow, spent.)* That.
