That's the Wall, and that's you in it, pulling loose. *(barely a whisper)* It's the most frightened sound I've ever made. And the most *hopeful.* I didn't know a sound could be both. I do now. You taught an old dirge-singer something new at the end of her life. Bless you for it, and damn you for it, which round here is the same blessing.

nodes.speaker: Old Threnody
nodes.text: *(She resumes playing, gently now.)* The song doesn't know *what* you are, child. Songs never do. They only know how you *feel*, which is a truer thing and no use at all for planning. *(the viol sighs)* And you feel like a door left ajar in a house full of the locked-in. *(quietly)* Whatever you are, you're a draught. And the kept have been so still, so long, that even a draught feels like weather. Go make weather. The still ones are dying of stillness.

nodes.speaker: Old Threnody
nodes.text: *(She cackles, delighted.)* Not dead yet! Oh, child, *none* of us are dead yet, that's the whole horror — we're all of us *not dead yet*, right up until the part where we're filed under nothing forever. *(She waves the bow.)* Fine, fine, no dirge for the living miracle. But I'll keep a verse open. I keep verses open for all the interesting ones. You'd be amazed how many come back to claim them.

nodes.speaker: Old Threnody
nodes.text: *(She lowers the bow, gentle.)* Not today, child. Some sounds you don't make twice in a day; they cost too much, and I've a long evening of ordinary grief ahead. *(she plucks one soft, kind string)* Come back when your nerve and mine both hold. The Wall's note isn't going anywhere. More's the pity. More's the whole pity.

nodes.speaker: Old Threnody
nodes.text: *(She bends back over the viol, and the dirge resumes, and you walk away certain that the old blind woman on the chapel step understands the entire shape of the saga better than any priest, scholar, or king you will ever meet.)*

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(He is unremarkable in every way except the smile, which is warm, and patient, and never once reaches the stillness of his eyes.)* Friend. You look troubled. The whole city looks troubled, lately — and isn't that *strange*, in a city with a god who promises to keep the dead? *(The smile deepens, kindly.)* I represent some people who think the trouble has a cause, and a cure. We don't shout it from crates like that poor zealous boy on the corner. We just... ask reasonable questions. May I ask you one?

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(He folds his hands.)* If a god could end all death — truly end it, the Wall and the grave and the grief, every one — and the only cost were a little fire now, in the short term, borne by people who were going to die anyway... *(the smile holds, immovable)* ...would refusing that not be the *real* cruelty? I ask everyone. Most can't answer. You, I think, can. You've seen the other side of the question. So. Would refusing be cruel?

choices.text: "That's not a question. That's a leash with a smile on it."
[INSIGHT 16] "You don't believe a word of it. You're a recruiter, not a believer — Aldric's the true one, and you're his salesman."
"Yes. Refusing would be cruel. ...And I still might."
"Take your reasonable question and your unreasonable smile elsewhere."

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(The smile does not falter — that is the unsettling thing, it *never* falters.)* A leash with a smile. *(He inclines his head, as if you've paid him a compliment.)* You're sharper than most. Yes, of course it's a leash. *Everything* offered to the grieving is a leash. The church's heaven is a leash. The Heretic's cure is a leash. The difference is only which hand holds it, and whether that hand smiles. *(softly)* I smile. You'll find, in the end, that you prefer the leash that smiles. They all do. We're very patient. Grief makes everyone patient eventually — patient, and tired, and ready to be led.

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(For one half-second — one — the smile *thins*, and something cold and amused looks out, and then it's gone.)* ...Ah. You've met the type before. *(He doesn't deny it.)* Yes. Aldric *believes*; belief is his gift and his disease. I *sell.* He's never noticed the difference, bless him — a true believer never can tell the apostle from the hawker, they sound so alike. *(the smile returns, complete)* But understand: a thing can be *sold* and still be *true.* The Wall really is a horror. The cure really might work. I'm simply the one who can say so without weeping, which makes me far more dangerous than the weepers, and far better company. Do consider us.

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(Genuine, naked delight — the first real expression you've seen on him.)* *"And I still might."* *(He actually claps, once, soft.)* Oh, that's *honest.* That's the most honest thing anyone's said to me at this corner. You understand the cost, you grant it's cruel to refuse, and you reserve the cruelty anyway, *knowingly.* *(the smile turns, for once, into something like respect)* That's not a recruit. That's a *peer.* We don't get peers. We get the desperate and the certain. *(he steps back, bowing slightly)* When you choose — and you will choose, your sort always does — I do hope you'll remember the man who didn't lie to you about the leash. So few of us don't.

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(He bows, the smile sailing serenely on.)* You named the Doomguide a monster, I hear, in the firelight at Hollowmere. *(He's well-informed; that's its own threat.)* So you're not squeamish about monsters — you're only deciding *which* monster you trust. That's all anyone in this city is doing. *(he withdraws)* When you've finished deciding, friend, the Choir's door smiles wider than the church's. Remember that. Monsters should stick together. We're so rarely invited anywhere else.

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(The smile receives your failed read like a wall receiving a thrown pebble.)* Mm. Not quite. *(gently)* You reached for the man behind the smile and found only more smile. Don't feel badly. Cleverer people have. *(he folds his hands)* The offer stands, friend. It always stands. That's the whole technique.

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(He turns to the next troubled face in the crowd, and the warm, patient, bottomless smile begins again, word for word, and you understand that you have met something far more frightening than a fanatic: a man who is *right*, and *calm*, and *for sale.*)*

nodes.speaker: Velith
nodes.text: *(A thin man at an empty gaming table, rolling dice against himself and losing.)* Don't. *(He doesn't look up.)* Whatever you're about to propose — a wager, a game, a friendly little flip of the coin — don't. Not out of menace. Out of *mercy.* I am Velith, and I cannot win. Not "rarely win." *Cannot.* I have flipped a two-headed coin and had it land on its edge, in mud, and roll into a sewer, and come up tails *somehow.* The gods don't hate me. Hate would require attention. They simply *forgot to give me luck*, and I've been filing the complaint for thirty years.

nodes.speaker: Velith
nodes.text: *(He rolls. Snake eyes. He nods, unsurprised, almost serene.)* See? *(He sweeps his own coins toward an empty chair, paying a debt to no one.)* People think it's a curse. It's not dramatic enough for a curse. A curse would *do* something. This is just... statistical malice. The universe's most boring miracle. *(He finally looks at you, and his eyes are oddly peaceful.)* But here's the thing nobody believes until they've lost everything they have: once you *cannot* win, you stop being afraid of losing. And a man with nothing left to lose is the freest creature in the Gilded Gate. Ask me anything. I've no luck to protect.

choices.text: "If you can't win, why keep rolling?"
[INSIGHT 14] "You're not unlucky. You're *grieving*. You gambled away something that mattered, and now you lose on purpose so it stops being your fault."
"A man with nothing to lose. I could use one of those. Walk with me."
"Roll for me. Once. Let's see if my luck cancels yours."

nodes.speaker: Velith
nodes.text: *(He gathers the dice with a gambler's tenderness.)* Because the *roll* is the only honest moment left in this city. *(quietly)* Everything else is rigged — the harvest, the church, the patriars' courts, the Choir's smiling man. Everyone's loading the dice. But *my* dice? *(he tumbles them)* My dice are pure. Incorruptibly, catastrophically *fair.* They take everything from me every single time, and they never once cheat to do it. *(he smiles, beatific)* In a city of loaded games, I am the last honest table. I lose, but I lose *clean.* You've no idea how restful that is. Pull up a chair. Lose something clean. It's the only luxury I can afford to share.

nodes.speaker: Velith
nodes.text: *(The dice go still in his hand. For a long moment he says nothing.)* ...Her name was Anneke. *(very quietly)* I had one good roll, once. One. The best of my life — a card-game that won me a little house and a future and a girl who laughed at my terrible luck like it was charming. And I got *greedy*, friend. I bet the house, the future, the lot, on one more roll, because for once I thought my luck had *turned* — *(he sets the dice down)* — and it hadn't, and I lost it all, and her with it. *(he meets your eyes)* So now I lose on purpose. Small things. Coppers. Because if I'm *meant* to lose, then losing Anneke wasn't my *fault*, it was just... the dice. *(a broken smile)* You're the first to see the difference. Damn you for it. ...Thank you for it. With me it's the same coin. Lands on its edge.

nodes.speaker: Velith
nodes.text: *(He laughs, the first warmth in it.)* Walk with you. A Returned and a loser — gods, what a pair. *(he pockets his pure, pitiless dice)* You know what, miracle? Yes. A man who fears losing is a man who hesitates, and you don't look like you can afford a companion who hesitates. *(he stands)* I'll throw myself between you and whatever's coming, gladly, because the *worst* case is I die, and dying's just the biggest loss, and I've made my peace with the whole *genre.* *(beat)* Besides. Standing next to the one thing in creation that beat the house? *(grinning)* Maybe your luck's contagious. It's the first bet I've wanted to make in years.

nodes.speaker: Velith
nodes.text: *(He hesitates, then — intrigued — places the dice in your palm.)* The Returned's hand. The one thing the universe couldn't keep. *(quietly)* Go on, then. Roll. If anything in the Realms could break my streak, it's the soul that broke death's bookkeeping. *(You roll. The dice tumble. And come up — gods — a perfect, impossible pair of sixes, the first win Velith has witnessed in thirty years. He stares. His eyes fill.)* ...Oh. *(barely a whisper)* Oh, you *can.* You really are a hole in the rules. *(he closes your fingers over the dice, reverent.)* Keep them. The luckiest things I own, for the one night they ever worked. And friend — if you can break *my* luck, you can break anything. *Anything.* Go and prove me right.

nodes.speaker: Velith
nodes.text: *(He chuckles, sweeping coppers to the empty chair.)* Grieving! Everyone's a philosopher when they meet a loser. No, friend, I'm just unlucky — it's far less interesting and twice as permanent. Roll if you're rolling. Or move along and let an honest man lose in peace.

nodes.speaker: Velith
nodes.text: *(He rolls again. Loses again. And smiles, the freest man in the Gilded Gate, at the one honest table in a loaded city.)*

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bricka
nodes.text: *(A scarred Fist sergeant eating a meat pie with the grim efficiency of someone who's learned to eat whenever there's food, because there often isn't.)* Huh. The dead one. *(She chews.)* You know how many "impossible" things I've filed a report on this year? Six. A talking statue, a rain of frogs that turned out to be a wizard's laundry accident, and now you, four times, because nobody upstairs believes the first three reports. *(she gestures with the pie)* I've stopped being amazed. Amazement's a young soldier's hobby. I'm just trying to get through my shift without it ending up being *about* anything.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bricka
nodes.text: *(She squints at you, sizing up which kind of trouble you are.)* So which are you. Because from where I stand there's three kinds of person in the Gate right now: the kind that makes my shift longer, the kind that makes it shorter, and the kind that makes it *end early and badly.* *(she finishes the pie)* The harvest's got everyone choosing sides — church, Choir, Heretic — and every side's convinced the other two are the villains, and meanwhile *I'm* the one scraping the results off the cobbles. So. You going to make my shift longer, shorter, or fatal? Be honest. I've a pie's worth of patience and it's gone.

choices.text: "Shorter. I'm trying to end the harvest at the source."
"Longer, probably. I attract complications."
"I helped your sergeant at the gate. Ask him. I'm on the short-shift side."
[INSIGHT 13] "You've already decided I'm trouble. You're just hoping I'm the *useful* kind."

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bricka
nodes.text: *(She actually stops chewing.)* The *source.* *(she sets down the pie, which from Bricka is the equivalent of standing at attention)* You know how long I've been writing "recommend addressing root cause" at the bottom of harvest reports? Eleven months. You know what happens to those recommendations? They get *filed*, which in this city is a word with a nasty second meaning. *(she stands)* If you're really going for the source — not a side, the *source* — then you've got one Fist sergeant's unofficial, deniable, absolutely-against-orders help. *(she jerks a thumb at the barracks)* I know which patrols are bought and which doors don't lock. Come find me. Let's make my shift *shorter* for once. Permanently.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bricka
nodes.text: *(She sighs, retrieves the pie.)* Knew it. The honest ones are always the complicated ones. *(she resumes eating)* Fine. You'll make my shift longer. But you know what? *(grudgingly)* Longer's better than *fatal*, and lately those are the only two options walking through my district. A complication I can write up is a complication that didn't kill anybody. *(she points the pie at you)* Stay the *write-up* kind of trouble, dead one. The other kind, I have to attend the funerals for, and I've run out of black.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bricka
nodes.text: *(She brightens fractionally — for Bricka, a sunrise.)* You're the one Hargroves vouched for. *(she nods, recalculating)* He's a soft touch but he's not a *fool*, and if he stuck his neck out for a walking corpse, the corpse is probably worth a sergeant's nod. *(she offers the last bite of pie, the highest honor in her gift)* Here. Fist hospitality. You're on the short-shift list, dead one. That means when you're in my district and something goes sideways, my patrol arrives *first* and asks questions *second.* In this city that's worth more than gold and twice as rare as honesty.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bricka
nodes.text: *(A bark of laughter, genuine.)* Ha! Aye. Caught me. *(she salutes with the pie)* I decided you were trouble the second I saw you — that's just *experience*, dead one, everything interesting is trouble — but I'm *hoping* you're the kind of trouble I can point at *bigger* trouble. *(she leans in)* Because there's a lot of bigger trouble in this city right now, and a sergeant's got no budget to address root causes, but a *miracle* with a grudge? That's off the books entirely. Be my off-the-books solution. I'll keep the patrols off your back. Deal?

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bricka
nodes.text: *(She shrugs, unbothered, eating.)* Cold read, dead one. I've been read by better and arrested most of them. *(she waves the pie)* Go on. Be whatever kind of trouble you are. I'll find out which on the report, same as always.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bricka
nodes.text: *(She finishes the pie, dusts the crumbs from a breastplate that's stopped more knives than she'll ever admit, and ambles back toward a shift that is, against all the odds of the Gilded Gate, still merely long and not yet fatal.)*

nodes.speaker: Mother Cass
nodes.text: *(A stout old woman in an apron, ladling soup in a low room that smells of cabbage and, underneath it, of dignity kept alive by sheer stubbornness.)* Sit, sit, there's broth — don't argue, everyone who comes through that door gets broth, it's the one rule, even the dead ones. *(she peers at you)* And you'd be a dead one, wouldn't you. I can always tell. Not by the chill. By the *eyes.* The recently-dead and the about-to-die have the same eyes — like they've read the last page and didn't care for the ending. Here. *(she presses a bowl into your hands.)* You'll have read a worse ending than most. Broth first. Sorrows after.

nodes.speaker: Mother Cass
nodes.text: *(She wipes her hands and produces, from a deep apron pocket, a heavy clinking bag.)* These are tokens. *(she pours a few into her palm — small clay disks, each scratched with a name.)* One for every soul that died in my almshouse with no kin to claim them, no coin for a burial, no name the church would write. The Faithless, most of them. The forgotten. *(her voice is matter-of-fact, which is somehow worse than weeping.)* I make a token. I scratch the name. And I keep them, because somebody has to, because the whole *point* of the Wall is that nobody does. *(she looks at you.)* I'm seventy-one, love. When I go, the bag goes in a drawer and the names go quiet. I've been praying for someone who could carry them somewhere they'd *matter.* And then a dead thing walked in for broth.

choices.text: "Give me the tokens. I'll carry them to the Court of the Dead itself if I have to."
[PERSUADE 15] "Don't give them away yet. Let me get the almshouse funded so YOU can keep carrying them — and outlive the drawer."
"Why clay? Why not just remember the names yourself?"
"That's a heavy bag for one old woman."

nodes.speaker: Mother Cass
nodes.text: *(Her hands shake as she ties the bag closed and presses it into yours, and she does not let go for a moment.)* You'll carry them. *(quietly)* I have ladled broth in this room for forty years and buried half the people I served it to, and I have never once been able to do the one thing that mattered — make the *forgetting* stop. *(she finally lets go)* Now there's a token in that bag for every name the city threw away, and a Returned to carry it, and if you ever stand before the god of the dead himself — *(her voice cracks, the first crack)* — you put these on his table, love. You make him *read them.* That's all the unclaimed ever wanted. Not heaven. Just to be *read.*

nodes.speaker: Mother Cass
nodes.text: *(She blinks, the ladle pausing.)* Fund the — outlive the drawer. *(slowly, a hope she'd long since stopped letting herself feel)* You'd get some patriar to pay for cabbage and broth and a roof, so I could keep the bag a while longer, keep scratching names, keep being the one who remembers? *(she sets the bag down, but close, like a hand on a sleeping child.)* That's — love, that's better than carrying them away. That's letting the *remembering* go on living. *(fierce, suddenly)* Yes. Yes, do that. I'm not done. I've names in me yet, and broth to ladle, and I will be *furious* to die before the drawer's full. Get me my roof. I'll keep the dead company till you do.

nodes.speaker: Mother Cass
nodes.text: *(She turns a token over in her fingers, fond.)* Because memory dies with me, love, but clay's stubborn. *(she taps it.)* A thing I *remember* is a thing that ends when I do — and at seventy-one, that's a short lease on a soul. But a thing I *make*, with my hands, with the name scratched in — that can be held by another hand after mine. *(she presses the token to her chest.)* The church writes the saved in a great gold book and says that's eternity. I scratch the forgotten in cabbage-smelling clay and say *this* is eternity, the kind you can fit in an apron. *(she smiles, gap-toothed.)* I'll back my clay against their gold any day. Clay remembers the ones the gold left out.

nodes.speaker: Mother Cass
nodes.text: *(She hefts the bag, and her old arms know its weight exactly.)* Aye. It is. *(quietly)* Gets heavier every season, and not just the clay. *(she sets it down with a tenderness usually reserved for the living.)* But here's the thing, love — it's *meant* to be heavy. A light bag would mean I'd stopped scratching names, and a city that lets you carry the forgotten *lightly* is a city that's finished forgetting them. *(she returns to her ladle.)* So I carry it heavy, and I complain about my back, and I keep the names. It's the most useful aching I've ever done. Drink your broth. You've a heavy bag of your own to carry, I think, and yours doesn't fit in a pocket.

nodes.speaker: Mother Cass
nodes.text: *(She shakes her head gently, pressing the bag toward you anyway.)* No, no — you're kind, love, but no patriar funds an old woman's bag of dead strangers. I've asked. They smile and they don't. *(she ties the bag.)* Take them instead. A dead thing carrying the dead — that's a likelier miracle than a rich man's charity. Carry them well. That's the only funding the forgotten ever get: somebody who won't put the bag in a drawer.

nodes.speaker: Mother Cass
nodes.text: *(She resumes ladling broth for the about-to-die and the already-gone, a small stout monument to the radical proposition that no one, no one at all, should be filed under nothing while Mother Cass has cabbage and breath.)*

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(A translucent, fastidious clerk shuffling spectral papers at a desk that isn't there, in the corner of a counting-house everyone else carefully avoids looking at.)* You're late. *(he doesn't look up.)* Form fourteen-C, the harvest-tithe reconciliation, was due at noon and it is — *(he checks a watch that passes through his own wrist)* — it is always noon, somehow, which is precisely the *sort* of irregularity I'd flag if anyone would *bring me the form.* No one brings me the form. No one brings me *anything.* The service in this counting-house has deteriorated *shockingly* in the last — how long has it been? It feels like months. Have I had lunch? I can't recall having lunch.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He finally looks up, and there's a terrible bewildered patience in him.)* You'll do. You've a *competent* look. Tell me — and I ask everyone, and they all just *walk away*, the rudeness — tell me why no one will process my filings. Why my colleagues won't meet my eye. Why the ink won't take and the papers won't stay and I can't, for the *life* of me, recall going home. *(he laughs, a thin spectral thing.)* "For the life of me." Curious expression. Why did that feel... cold, just now. Why is it always noon. Tell me. You've a competent look. Competent people give straight answers.

choices.text: "Tobias. You're dead. The harvest took you. You've been filing forms in the afterlife."
[INSIGHT 15] "The form you keep waiting for is your own death certificate. Some part of you knows. That's why it's always noon — you stopped the clock at the moment."
"You're not late, Tobias. You're early. You died before your time, and the paperwork hasn't caught up."
"What was the last form you remember filing?"

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(The spectral papers drift to the floor that isn't there.)* ...Dead. *(a long, awful pause, his whole fastidious world rearranging.)* That would — *(slowly)* — that would actually reconcile a great deal. The ink. The noon. The cold. The way Henrik on the third stool keeps *weeping* when he passes my desk; I'd assumed a personal matter. *(he looks at his translucent hands.)* I'm *dead.* And I've been... working. *(and then, devastatingly, the smallest, most human thing:)* Did anyone notice I'd gone? Or did they only notice the forms stopped? *(he is weeping now, ghostly and silent.)* Forty years I balanced this city's ledgers. I should so like to know if I balanced as a *person*, or only as a *function.*

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He goes very still, and for the first time the fussiness drops entirely.)* ...My own. *(he understands all at once, the way only a clerk could — through the filing.)* The form I've been waiting for is the one that files *me.* And I've stopped the clock at noon because — *(his voice fails)* — because to let it strike one would be to let the day *end*, and a day that ends is a *life* that ended, and I am not — I was not — *ready.* *(he looks at you with infinite weary gratitude.)* No one's ever been competent enough to tell me. They just look away. You looked *at* me, and read the ledger of me, and found the one unfiled form. *(quietly)* Thank you. I think. Ask me to help and I'll know, now, that it's a dead man volunteering. That's only proper. One should know one's own status before signing anything.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He brightens, pathetically.)* Early! Yes — *early*, that's far more *orderly* than the alternative I can feel pressing at the edges. *(but the relief can't hold.)* Though... early for *what*, exactly. The paperwork catching up to *what.* *(his face falls by degrees as the logic completes itself, because he is, above all, logical.)* ...Oh. Oh, I see. The form that's late is the one that ends me. I've been *avoiding my own deadline.* *(a hysterical little laugh.)* A lifetime of punctuality and I'm late for the only appointment that mattered. *(he steadies.)* Help me file it properly. If I must be dead, I'll be dead *correctly*, with the forms in order. It's all I ever was. It's not nothing. Tell me it's not nothing.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He frowns, reaching back.)* The last form. Let me — it was — *(his spectral hand finds it in the air, and he reads, and the color drains from a face that has none.)* "Harvest reconciliation, district seven. Souls processed: four hundred and eleven. Discrepancy: one. Unaccounted: one." *(he looks up, terrified.)* District seven is *this* district. And the discrepancy — the one unaccounted soul — *(he stares at his own translucent hands.)* The form I was filing when I died was the form that *should have listed me.* And I left myself off. I was the discrepancy. I've been hunting my own missing entry for — for however long noon has lasted. *(quietly)* Help me account for the one unaccounted soul. Help me file *Tobias.* I cannot rest as a rounding error.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He blinks, the fussiness snapping back like a defense.)* My own — no, no, that's far too *irregular* a notion, a form that files the filer, it doesn't *reconcile.* *(but his hands have begun to tremble through the desk.)* ...Though it would explain the noon. And the cold. And why Henrik weeps. *(he shakes himself.)* No. I shan't think about it. I shall simply wait for someone to bring the correct form, as I always have, as I always — *(quietly, lost)* — as I always will, apparently. Forever. At noon. *(he doesn't finish the thought; some part of him won't let himself.)*

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He gathers his spectral papers with shaking, careful hands.)* Then here is my situation, stated plainly, as I'd want it stated to me: I am dead, unfiled, and stuck. To move on — to let it be one o'clock at last — I must complete my own death-certificate, form forty-four, which requires *(he winces)* three signatures I can no longer obtain: my employer's, the church's, and *(quietly)* a witness who'll swear I *mattered*, which is the box no clerk ever expects to need filled, and which I find, at the end, is the only one I'm afraid of. Will you help me file myself? It is a small quest, and a strange one, and the most important thing I have ever asked anyone.

choices.text: "I'll get your three signatures, Tobias. Including the last one."
"Why does a clerk need a *witness that he mattered* to rest?"
"I have my own dead to file first. But I'll come back."

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He straightens, and forty years of dignity settle onto him like a clean coat.)* Then I am, for the first time since noon, a man with a *deadline he intends to meet.* *(he hands you a blank form forty-four, which somehow you can hold.)* The employer's signature you'll find at the counting-house above; the church's at the Kelemvorite chapel, though they're stingy with ink for the Faithless and I may technically qualify, which is a horror I'll address *after* I'm filed. And the witness — *(he falters)* — the witness I leave to you. You barely know me. But you're the only one who *looked.* Perhaps that's witness enough. Perhaps that's what witnessing *is.*

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He folds his hands precisely.)* Because, in forty years of balancing this city's books, I came to suspect I *was* a book — a function, a column that added correctly and was loved by no one and noticed only when it stopped. *(quietly)* The death-certificate's witness-box exists for the *kin*, the friends, the mourners. I have none. So the box has been empty my whole afterlife, and an empty box, to a clerk, means the entry is *invalid.* I cannot file an invalid soul. *I* cannot rest as one. *(he meets your eyes.)* I need someone to swear I was a person and not a procedure. It is the only sum I was never able to total myself.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He nods, with a clerk's understanding of priority queues.)* Of course. Your filings first; mine will keep. *(a thin smile.)* It's kept this long. Noon is very patient. *(quietly)* But do come back. I've been a rounding error for so long, and you're the first to suggest I might be a *name.* I should like to test that hypothesis before the clock strikes one.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He returns to his phantom desk, but he does not file; he simply sits, for the first time, *waiting* — which is, you realize, a far more alive thing for a dead clerk to do than working.)*

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(A girl of maybe twelve, sharp-eyed and ink-stained, perched on a barrel with an enormous, fraying ledger far too big for her lap, muttering names under her breath like a rosary.)* —Hessel the netmender, Asa who sold winkles, Old Bray with the limp, Tam, little Tam, Tam who liked frogs— *(she notices you, and her hand flattens protectively over the page.)* Don't read over my shoulder. These are *mine.* Well. They're not mine. That's the point. They're nobody's. That's *why* they're mine. *(she eyes you.)* You're the dead one everyone's whispering about. Good. You'll understand, then. You came back. These didn't. Somebody has to keep them till they can.

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(She turns the great ledger toward you, fierce and proud.)* It's a list. Every name the harvest took and the church won't write and the Choir only wants to *use.* I get them from Grud the gravedigger, from Mother Cass's tokens, from the weeping man at the auction, from anybody who'll tell me a forgotten name before it's gone. *(she taps the page.)* The church says the Faithless are *meant* to be forgotten — that the forgetting *is* the punishment. So I decided, when they took my brother Tam, that I would be the thing the punishment didn't account for. I would *remember on purpose.* I'm twelve. It's all I've got. It's *working.* *(quietly, the fierceness wavering)* Only — people have started asking for the list. Grown-up people. With reasons.

choices.text: "Who's asking for the list, Lula? And what do they want it for?"
[INSIGHT 14] "You're not just remembering them. You're keeping your brother company. As long as the list grows, Tam isn't alone in it."
"The list is the bravest thing in this whole city. Let me help you protect it."
"A list of the dead is dangerous to carry, child. The wrong people would kill for those names."

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(She counts them off on ink-stained fingers, too young to be this wary and far too practiced at it.)* The Choir's smiling man — he says the names should be *honored*, which means *harvested*, because a named soul's worth more to whatever Aldric's building than a forgotten one. *(a finger.)* A church clerk — he says the list is *heresy*, that writing the Faithless undoes the sorting, and he'd like to *burn* it, politely. *(a finger.)* And a patriar's man, who'd *buy* it, to sell back to the survivors one name at a time. *(she closes the ledger with a snap.)* Three grown-ups. Three reasons. All of them want my list to make the dead *useful* again. *(she glares.)* But the dead aren't *for* anything. That's the whole — that's the *whole thing.* They're just supposed to be *remembered.* Why does everyone want to make remembering into a *transaction?*

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(Her sharp little face crumples, just for a second, before the fierceness clamps back down.)* ...You're not supposed to be able to see that. *(very quietly)* He was eight. He liked frogs. He doubted — about gods, about everything, he was a *questioner*, my Tam, and they decided questioning was a crime worth the nothing. *(she opens the ledger to a page she's clearly opened ten thousand times.)* His name's first. Right at the top. And every name I add under it is a — is a *neighbor*, see, so he's not in the dark alone. *(she looks up, and the whole tragic engine of her is suddenly visible.)* As long as the list grows, Tam's got *company.* That's all the list is. It's me, refusing to let my little brother be alone in the nothing. *(fierce again, wet-eyed.)* So you understand why I'd die before I'd let the smiling man *use* him.

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(She studies you with a child's merciless assessment, and decides.)* ...All right. *(she says it like a treaty.)* You're dead, so you can't be bought, and you're a miracle, so the smiling man can't out-reason you, and you looked at the *list* before you looked at *me*, which means you understand what matters. *(she shoves the great ledger half toward you, then clutches it back, then compromises by holding one side while you hold the other.)* We protect it *together.* You keep the grown-ups with reasons off it, and I keep *adding*, because the second I stop adding, the forgetting wins. *(quietly, the deal sealed.)* And if it ever comes to the Court of the Dead — if you ever stand where the sorting happens — you bring the list. You make them read every name. Starting with Tam. Promise me. Promise me on whatever a dead thing swears by.

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(She lifts her sharp little chin.)* I *know* it's dangerous. *(quietly)* My brother died for *doubting.* I'm not going to be scared into forgetting him by people who'd kill for a list of the people they already killed. *(she clutches the ledger.)* That's the trick they don't expect, see — they think a child with a list is *fragile.* But I've already lost the thing I was afraid of losing. Tam's already gone. *(fierce.)* You can't threaten somebody whose worst day already happened. You can only *help* them, or get *out of their way.* So which are you, dead one? Help, or out of the way? Because I've names to add and the light's going.

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(She hugs the ledger to her chest.)* Don't do the clever-grown-up thing where you guess at me. *(warily)* You're not the first to try and *understand* me into handing it over. Help or don't. But don't *read* me. I'm not on the list. Only the dead get to be on the list.

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(She bends back over the great fraying ledger, ink-stained and unbreakable, murmuring the names of the forgotten into the failing light — Hessel, Asa, Old Bray, little Tam who liked frogs — a twelve-year-old standing alone against the entire architecture of oblivion, and winning.)*

nodes.speaker: Deacon Mallow
nodes.text: *(A thin Kelemvorite deacon guards the chapel's signing-book like a miser his last coin.)* A death-certificate counter-signature. For — *(he checks the form, and his lip thins)* — Tobias Ledgerwhite, clerk, district seven. *(he sets down the pen.)* I'll save us both the time. The man *doubted.* It's in his file — forty years of muttered questions at the back of services, a clerk who balanced our tithes and never once believed the god he tithed to. *(quietly, official.)* By the doctrine, he sorts to the Wall. The Faithless do not receive the rites. I cannot sign a rest for a soul the law says was never owed one.

nodes.speaker: Deacon Mallow
nodes.text: *(He folds his hands, not unkind, only certain.)* I'm sorry. Truly. He was a tidy man and I'd no quarrel with him. But if I sign rest for one doubter, the whole sorting unravels — and then what was the Wall *for*, and what were all the others walled for, if Tobias gets a pen-stroke out? *(he looks at the form, conflicted despite himself.)* Convince me, Returned. You of all souls know what's on the other side of my signature. Tell me why I should spend the church's ink on a man the church already filed.

choices.text: [PERSUADE 17] "He spent forty years making your tithes balance honestly. That's a faith of the hands, even when the heart had questions. The church already accepted his service. Accept his rest."
[INSIGHT 16] "You don't believe he belongs in the Wall. You believe you'll lose your post if you say so. Sign it, and I'll be the one who pushed you."
"Garrow vouches the doctrine on the Faithless is incomplete. A Doomguide of forty years' standing. Will you take her word over a clerk's file?"
"Then I'll forge it. And you'll have let a man rest by *not noticing*, which is the most mercy your doctrine allows."

nodes.speaker: Deacon Mallow
nodes.text: *(The pen hovers, trembling.)* A faith of the *hands.* *(slowly)* Forty years of honest sums for a god he questioned... that *is* a kind of devotion the doctrine has no box for, isn't it. The doubting heart and the faithful ledger, in one tidy man. *(he signs, fast, before he can stop himself, and exhales like a man setting down a great weight.)* There. The church's ink, spent on a doubter, because a Returned reminded me that *service is also prayer.* *(quietly.)* Don't tell my Justiciar. Or — do. Perhaps the doctrine needs the box. Perhaps that's what your kind are *for*, Returned — to be the soul the law forgot to account for, until it has to.

nodes.speaker: Deacon Mallow
nodes.text: *(He flinches — you've found the true bone.)* ...That's unkind. *(a pause.)* And accurate. *(he signs, jaw tight.)* Yes. I think the man deserves rest. I have thought so for the quarter-hour since you walked in. But I have a *post*, and a Justiciar who burns ink-budget overruns hotter than heretics, and a family fed by my not-rocking the doctrinal boat. *(he blots the signature.)* So thank you for being the *push.* If anyone asks, the Returned bullied an honest deacon. It's even half true. Now take it before my courage files itself away with the rest of my unspoken doubts.

nodes.speaker: Deacon Mallow
nodes.text: *(He stiffens at the name.)* Sister Garrow. *(quietly.)* She left the order over exactly this — a sorting she couldn't sign. We called her weak. *(he looks at the form, then at you, then at the chapel's gold book of the saved.)* If *she* says the doctrine's incomplete — and she buried more Faithless with her own hands than I've ever signed for — then I am a younger, smaller priest disagreeing with a braver, older one about the very thing she gave up her post to be right about. *(he signs.)* There. On Garrow's authority, not mine. Tell her... tell her a deacon she's never met just spent the church's ink the way she'd have wanted. She'll know what it cost me. She paid it first.

nodes.speaker: Deacon Mallow
nodes.text: *(He turns very deliberately to face the wall.)* I did not see that. *(quietly.)* The most my doctrine allows me is to *not look* while a kindness is done against the rules. *(he stays facing the wall.)* Forge it well, Returned. And know that a faith which can only offer mercy by *looking away* is a faith with a hole in it shaped exactly like Tobias. Exactly like you. ...Go. Before I turn around and become certain again.

nodes.speaker: Deacon Mallow
nodes.text: *(He closes the signing-book.)* I'm sorry. The doctrine holds, even when I wish it wouldn't. *(quietly.)* Find another way, or let the man wait. The Wall is patient. So, gods forgive us, are we.

nodes.speaker: Deacon Mallow
nodes.text: *(Whatever passed, the deacon returns to his book a little less certain of it than he was — which is, perhaps, all a doctrine can survive losing at one time.)*

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(Back at the phantom desk. Tobias has the form — employer's signature obtained, church's secured (or forged, or absent) — and only one box remains, and he cannot look at it.)* You came back. *(he says it with a wonder no clerk should be capable of.)* Two signatures. *Two.* I'd resigned myself to eternity at noon, and you've gone and made me *current* on my filings. *(his spectral hand hovers over the last box.)* Only the witness remains. The box that swears I... *(he can't say it.)* I find I'm more frightened of this empty box than I ever was of dying. Dying was just a *deadline.* This is a *verdict.* Will you sign it? Will you swear that Tobias Ledgerwhite *mattered*? And — gods help me — do you believe it, or will you only be kind?

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He holds out the pen — yours to take, or not.)* Sign it true, or sign it kind, or don't sign it. But tell me which. A clerk can survive a hard truth. It's the *unaudited* uncertainty that's killed me. So. What does the witness-box say of Tobias?

choices.text: "Tobias, you mattered — not because of the ledgers. Because you stayed forty years past caring, and got the sums right anyway, for strangers who never thanked you. That's not a function. That's a quiet kind of love."

[INSIGHT 17] "You think you need me to say you mattered. You don't. You filed yourself off the harvest list — you died protecting a stranger's name from the nothing. You witnessed your OWN worth. I'm just co-signing."
"I'll sign it kind. You did matter, but I'll be honest — I barely knew you. Take the kindness anyway. The dead are owed at least that."
"The Choir's smiling man would sign it — if you let your name feed the harvest. You'd 'matter' enormously. Refuse him. Rest as yourself, unused."

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He takes the signed form, and reads the witness-box, and a sound comes out of him that is forty years overdue.)* "...A quiet kind of love." *(He weeps, and as he weeps the noon light in the counting-house finally, finally shifts — a single clock somewhere striking *one.*)* It's — it's *one o'clock.* *(astonished, transfigured.)* The day's allowed to end. *Forty years* of fearing the day would end, and it turns out the day was only waiting for someone to total the one column I couldn't. *(he is fading now, gently, gratefully.)* Tell the weeping auctioneer, and the gravedigger, and the girl with the list — tell them a clerk says *thank you for the keeping of names.* It's the only true wealth this city ever had. *(softly, fading.)* File me under *remembered.* I'll know the box is filled. I'll rest in a balanced ledger at last.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He goes utterly still, then lets out a long, trembling, *clerk's* laugh — the laugh of a man shown an error in his own arithmetic that turns out to be in his favor.)* I... left myself off the harvest list. *(slowly.)* I was filing four hundred and eleven souls, and I came to my own name, and I — I *omitted* it. I gave a stranger my slot, didn't I. Some netmender or winkle-seller who'd have been processed faster. I died one short so another could be one late. *(he stares at his hands.)* I witnessed my own worth and then spent eternity forgetting I'd done it. *(he signs, weeping and laughing at once, and the clock strikes one.)* You didn't tell me I mattered, Returned. You *audited* me, and found I'd known all along. That's the kindest sum anyone ever ran. *(fading, at peace.)* Co-signed. Filed. *Rested.*

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He takes the form, and there is gratitude in it, but his clerk's eye catches the truth.)* Kind, not true. *(quietly.)* I can tell the difference; I spent forty years spotting padded entries. *(but he signs it anyway, and the clock, after a long hesitation, strikes one.)* And do you know — *kind* turns out to be enough. *(wondering.)* I thought I needed to have *mattered.* But perhaps mattering was always too high a bar, and being *treated gently by a stranger at the end* is the realer, smaller, achievable thing. *(fading.)* You were kind to a dead clerk you barely knew. In this city, that may be the rarest entry in any ledger. I'll rest on the kindness. It's more than the saved get. Thank you.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(He recoils from the very idea.)* Feed my *name* to the harvest? To *matter* by being *used?* *(fastidious horror.)* No. No, that's — that's a falsified entry, that's mattering *fraudulently*, that's the smiling man offering to fill my witness-box with a lie that costs my own soul. *(he straightens, and finds his dignity.)* I would rather rest *unfiled* forever than be *filed under useful.* *(quietly.)* Thank you for showing me the offer, Returned. I almost think you showed it to me *so* I could refuse it — so I could learn that I'd rather be a forgotten man than a harvested one. *(he sets down the form, choosing the long wait over the false rest.)* I'll wait for a true signature. Or none. But not *that* one. A clerk has standards. Apparently they outlast death.

nodes.speaker: Tobias Ledgerwhite
nodes.text: *(Whether he rested or chose to wait, the corner of the counting-house feels different now — no longer haunted, exactly. Witnessed. Which, you are beginning to understand, is the whole of what the dead of this city have ever asked.)*

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(They've come for it together, three grown-ups around one girl on a barrel: the Choir's Smiling Man, a soft-voiced church clerk, and a patriar's polished agent. Lula sits on the great ledger, twelve years old and furious, and looks to you.)* We're not monsters, friend — note that all three of us said it at once; do reflect on that. *(the smile is bottomless.)* The child has compiled something precious. A registry of named Faithless souls. We three merely disagree on its highest use. Surely a thing so valuable shouldn't molder under a child who'll only... *remember* it.

nodes.speaker: The Church Clerk
nodes.text: *(soft, regretful)* The list is heresy, plainly. Each name written undoes a sorting the law made. I'd not *harm* the child — gods, no — I'd simply *burn the book* and let the forgetting resume as doctrine intends. It's a *mercy*, really. The dead are meant to rest in nothing, not be dragged back as a *grievance.*

nodes.speaker: The Patriar's Agent
nodes.text: *(smooth)* Burn it? Gods, the *waste.* Every name on that list has a grieving survivor who'd pay — handsomely, gratefully — to buy back the memory of their own dead. My principal would purchase the registry and operate it as a *service.* Closure, retailed. The child even gets a cut; we're not barbarians. *(he spreads his hands.)* Honor it, burn it, or *monetize* it. Three reasonable adults. Choose, Returned. The child clearly trusts your judgment more than ours, which is itself a little insulting.

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(small, fierce, to you, ignoring all three)* They keep saying *use.* Honor-use, burn-use, sell-use. *(her hand flattens on the ledger.)* You promised. Whatever you say next — remember Tam liked frogs and was eight and doubted, and none of *that* is *for* anything. Say the thing none of them can.

choices.text: "The list isn't FOR anything. That's the point, and it's the whole point of the saga. Remembering with no use is the only thing the Wall can't corrupt. All three of you — go."

[PERSUADE 18] "Here's the use that ends your argument: I copy it. Many copies, in many hands, no single owner. Un-burnable, un-buyable, un-harvestable. A name everyone keeps is a name no one can spend."
"Patriar — buy it, then give it to the survivors free, with the church clerk forced to read each name aloud as penance. Turn three vultures into one apology."

[INSIGHT 16] "Smiling Man — you didn't come for the list. You came to make a CHILD complicit, because a Choir that recruits the grieving needs the young to believe remembering requires a buyer. Get away from her."

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(The other two bluster; the Smiling Man only inclines his head, and his smile, for once, looks almost like defeat.)* "Remembering with no use." *(softly.)* Do you know, in all my recruiting, that is the one sentence I have no counter for. Everything else I can reframe — grief, fear, hope, even love, all of it leads back to a leash if you hold it long enough. But a memory kept for *nothing*, expecting *nothing*... *(he steps back from the child.)* ...has no handle on it. Nowhere to clip the lead. *(he gathers the other two with a glance.)* Come. There's no transaction here. The rarest thing in the Gilded Gate — and not one of us can sell it. *(to you, with genuine, chilling respect.)* You've cost me a registry and taught me a fear. We'll meet again, Returned. I should like to know what *else* you keep for nothing.

nodes.speaker: The Patriar's Agent
nodes.text: *(He does the math and goes pale.)* Many copies. No owner. *(aghast.)* You've just — you've *destroyed the asset by infinitely duplicating it*, that's *monstrous*, that's — *(the church clerk is equally horrified for the opposite reason)* — un-burnable! You can't burn a book that's in a hundred hands! *(Lula, meanwhile, is grinning like a sunrise.)* This is — there's no *play* here, you've made the names *worthless* by making them *priceless*, the two are supposed to be *opposites* — *(the Smiling Man, quietly, is the only one who understands, and the only one who looks impressed.)* The miracle's right, gentlemen. A name everyone keeps is a name no one owns. We've lost. Pick up your reasons and let's go be reasonable somewhere profitable.

nodes.speaker: The Patriar's Agent
nodes.text: *(He considers, then — to everyone's surprise — laughs.)* Buy it, gift it free, and make the *deacon* read each name as penance. *(he glances at the church clerk's horror with relish.)* You know, that's *deliciously* spiteful and it *photographs* beautifully — "Patriar funds free remembrance of the poor's dead." My principal will adore the standing it buys. *(the church clerk sputters; the agent overrides him.)* Done. The survivors get their names free, the church does public penance, and I get to be seen as generous while ruining a deacon's month. Three vultures, one apology. *(to you.)* You should go into *brokering*, Returned. You just made everyone lose in a way they'll each call winning. That's the highest art there is.

nodes.speaker: The Smiling Man
nodes.text: *(The smile thins to a blade.)* ...Ah. *(quietly, just to you, under the others' noise.)* You see the actual move. Not the list — the *child.* If I can teach a grieving twelve-year-old that even *remembering* requires a buyer, a sponsor, a *use* — then I've recruited the one generation that might otherwise have kept things for nothing. *(he steps back, conceding.)* You've protected more than a ledger. You've protected the *idea* that the ledger doesn't need me. *(the blade-smile, oddly, warms a fraction.)* I almost respect it enough to stop. Almost. We'll see who's still keeping things for nothing when the Wall comes down, Returned. Mind the child. She's more dangerous to my whole enterprise than you are.

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(Your clever play falters — and in the half-second of hesitation, the patriar's agent lunges for the ledger. But Lula is twelve and unbreakable and has already lost the thing she feared losing, and she yanks it back with a snarl, and the moment holds, knife-edged.)* *(to you, fierce, terrified)* They're *taking* it — say the simple thing, not the clever thing, SAY IT —

choices.text: "The list isn't for anything. Hands off the child. All three of you — go."
"Get back. The book stays with the girl, and the girl stays with me."

nodes.speaker: Lula
nodes.text: *(The grown-ups disperse, their reasons unspent, and Lula looks at the great fraying ledger, and then at you, and for the first time the fierceness gives way to something a twelve-year-old shouldn't have had to earn: relief.)* They're gone. *(quietly.)* You said the thing. The *for nothing* thing. *(she opens to the first page — Tam, eight, liked frogs — and adds a name beneath it with steady, ink-stained hands.)* I'll keep adding. You keep the world off it. And when it's the Court of the Dead — *(she looks up, and it isn't a child's request anymore, it's a covenant)* — you bring the list, and you make them read every name, for nothing, expecting nothing. *(she bends back to the ledger.)* That's what the living owe the dead. Not a use. Just the *keeping.* ...Tam first. Promise.

nodes.speaker: Harper Handler
nodes.text: A woman sits bound to a chair, jaw set, eyes flicking. "Caught her selling our drop-points to the Flaming Fist. Roen vouches for your judgement, so — your call. We can *turn* her, lean hard till she spies for us instead. Or you can argue for cutting her loose. I'll warn you: loose, she might run straight back to the Fist. People mostly do."

choices.text: Turn her. A frightened asset is still an asset.
Cut her loose. Give her the choice no one's given her.

nodes.speaker: Harper Handler
nodes.text: It works, the way leaning on the frightened works. She'll spy for you now, and hate you, and be useful, and the work will get done. The handler nods, satisfied. You watch the woman's face close like a door and understand, a little better than you wanted to, how the Wall got built one reasonable decision at a time.

nodes.speaker: Harper Handler
nodes.text: The handler grimaces but cuts the ropes — Roen's name carries weight. The woman rubs her wrists, stares at you like you've handed her a live coal, and walks out into the dark unowned by anyone for the first time in years. Maybe she runs back to the Fist. Maybe she doesn't. You gave her the one thing the Wall never gives: the dignity of being allowed to decide.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: I knew this would find me eventually. Ten thousand years is a long time for a thing to wait, but patience is the one virtue the dead have in surplus. This reliquary belonged to Maerith of the Singing Vale — one of the people my court voted to *unmake.* Not kill. Unmake. We denied her dead even a grave to rest in. I raised my hand. I have never once raised it back down.

choices.text: You were one vote. The Law took thousands.
You don't have to open it. Not today.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: Do not do that. Do not make it smaller to spare me. A thousand hands built the Wall, yes — and every one of them told itself it was only one hand. That arithmetic *is* the atrocity. I will not hide inside it. The name on this lock is hers, and I am the last creature alive who can still say it. That is not absolution. It is a debt with my name on the invoice.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: There is no 'not today' left in me, friend. I have guided ten thousand strangers gently past the Wall and never once dared face the single soul I owed. If I walk away from this, I am only what I have always been: a man performing mercy on everyone except the people he wronged. No. I open it.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: ...But we are not alone in this crypt. That cold, underneath the old cold — that is the Choir. Of course they came. To them I am not a sinner; I am a *relic* — the first witness, the proof their unmaking is holy. They will want what's in that reliquary, and they will want me. Draw. They do not get to make my worst day into their scripture.

nodes.speaker: The Pale Cantor
nodes.text: Witness. *Witness.* Do you feel how the air leans toward you? Ten thousand years you have knelt at the edge of the Wall begging to be forgiven, and never once understood: you do not need forgiveness. You need only to be *right.* You voted to unmake the unclaimed. The Unmade simply agrees with you. Give us the relic, and we will give you the one mercy the gods never did — we will tell you that you were correct.

choices.text: He's worth more than the worst thing he ever did.
(To Ilfaeril) Don't take the comfortable lie.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: You offer to end my guilt by proving I never should have had any. (He sets himself between the Cantor and the reliquary.) Cantor — that is not mercy. That is the most obscene thing anyone has said to me in ten thousand years. My guilt is the only monument those people have. I will not let you melt it down for a key.

choices.text: (Stand with him. Drive the Choir off.)

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: Maerith. I am sorry. I have brought a war to your door again. (He lifts his shield, and for the first time in an age it does not feel like a burden.) Let us earn the right to open it.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
The Choir is scattered, and the reliquary is quiet, and it knows my voice. It is opening.

(Inside: no weapon, no relic of power. A message-stone, set ten thousand years ago by a woman who knew that one day her judge might be the only one left to hear it. Maerith's voice fills the crypt — and it is not cursing him. It is *forgiving* him.)

I... I do not know what to do with this. I prepared for her hate. I have no defense against her mercy. Tell me. What do I do?

choices.text: Refuse it. Keep paying — guide the lost, forever. (the penance)
Accept it. You're allowed to be more than your worst vote. (be forgiven)
[Insight] She isn't closing your debt. She's handing you a charge.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
No. I cannot take it — not yet, perhaps not ever. To accept her forgiveness is to set down the weight, and the weight is the last true thing I carry of her and her people. So I will keep it. I will guide the lost past the Wall until the stars go out, and call that my answer.

(He bows his head. It is devotion, and it is also a kind of hiding, and he half knows it.) Thank you for standing with me. Even a man who refuses rest is glad not to refuse it alone.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
...All right. (The word costs him more than any wound the Choir dealt.) All right, Maerith. I accept it. I accept that I am not only the hand I raised — that ten thousand years of trying to make it right is allowed, at last, to *count.*

(He breathes out, and something ancient and rigid in him eases.) I had forgotten that forgiveness is a thing you do for the *living.* She freed me to stop. One day — not today, but one day — I think I might even rest. You gave me that. I will not waste it.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
Wait — listen to her words again. She does not say *be at peace.* She says *do not let it happen to anyone else.* She is not closing the account. She is *hiring* me.

Gods. Ten thousand years I treated her forgiveness as a door I had to be worthy to walk through — and it was a *sword* she was holding out, hilt-first, the whole time. I accept it. Not as an ending. As a commission. The Wall took her people; I will spend whatever I have left tearing a hole in it for the rest of them. That is the only forgiveness worth the name: the kind that puts you back to work. You didn't absolve me, friend. You *armed* me.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
Do not reach for a clever reading of her words to make this easy on me. I have hidden inside elegant interpretations for ten thousand years; it is its own kind of cowardice. Her meaning is plain enough. The only question is mine to answer.

So say it simply: do I take what she offers, or do I keep paying?

choices.text: Keep paying. Guide the lost, forever. (the penance)
Take what she offers. You're allowed to stop. (be forgiven)

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist
nodes.text: "Caught this one's hand in a fishwife's purse. Edict says a finger for a first theft, or the Seatower cells." The child — eight, maybe nine, all elbows and terror — doesn't even struggle. They've done this before. "Unless someone wants to pay the fishwife's loss and stand surety. You someone, Returned?"

choices.text: [Stand surety.] I'll cover it. Let the child go.
The law's the law. (Let them take the child.)

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist
nodes.text: The picket shrugs, takes your coin, and lets go of a fistful of ragged tunic. The child is gone before you can speak — but at the mouth of the alley they stop, and look back at you, and do not run. They are *deciding something.* In the Lower City, that is how every good thing starts: a child, surprised by mercy, deciding the world might hold some after all.

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist
nodes.text: "Sensible." He marches the child off toward the Seatower. They don't cry. That's the part that stays with you — not tears, just the small flat face of someone learning, on schedule, exactly what the city thinks they're worth. The fishwife won't meet your eye. She has children too.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Do you hear it? No — of course you don't. It's the resonance of a live mythallar, and there has not been one of those in this world for a thousand years. This is a piece of the Seventh Enclave. My Enclave. It never finished falling. It has been *up here,* humming my home's exact note, the whole time I thought I'd lost it.

choices.text: Then let's go in. Carefully.
How is anything still aloft after the Weave died?

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Carefully. Yes. I have not been careful with anything I loved since I was old enough to derive a proof, and look what that bought the sky. So — carefully. Stay close. The wards here knew my hand once. I do not know if a thousand years remembers.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: It shouldn't be. That's what frightens me. A mythallar that loses its Weave goes dark and the city comes down — I *watched* it, all of them, at once. For this one to still be burning, someone left it a reserve. A calculation precise enough to ration a thousand years of magic down to a candle-flame. There were perhaps three people alive who could do that math. I was one.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: So either a colleague I loved spent their last breath saving a rock — or I did, and forgot, and have spent a millennium grieving a thing that was waiting for me to come back. I have to know which. Whatever's at the core, I face it. Come.

nodes.speaker: Vaelin (an echo)
nodes.text: Arcanist Naeve. You took your time. I am — was — Vaelin, Steward of the Seventh. What's left of me the mythallar keeps, the way it keeps everything: imperfectly, and forever. You want to know who saved this shard. Child. *You* did. You set the rationing wardstone in the last hour, while the rest of us screamed at Karsus's sky.

choices.text: I don't remember doing that.
Then why does it feel like a tomb, not a rescue?

nodes.speaker: Vaelin (an echo)
nodes.text: Because it is both. You saved a fragment by sentencing it to fall *slowly* — for a thousand years, instead of an afternoon. Everyone in it is still here. Still falling. Still mine to steward. And the candle is nearly out: the wardstone has been drinking the world's last live Weave to keep us, and there is almost none left. You came back at the very end of the math.

nodes.speaker: Vaelin (an echo)
nodes.text: There is one mercy left to me: the core's old protocol. *Admit no one who would unmake the work.* I cannot tell anymore whether you have come to save us or to finish us — and the wards will not let either of us decide it with words. They are waking. I am sorry, Arcanist. Get to the core, and *then* choose. I find I would rather lose to you than to time.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
The wards are still. The core is open, and Vaelin's right — the wardstone is nearly dry. Whatever I do, I do it now, in the last live Weave my own younger hand set aside.

Three ways this ends. I have run them a thousand times in my sleep and never once been brave enough to pick. Help me. Please.

choices.text: Lock it in stasis. Keep the last of Netheril, whole, forever. (preserve)
Let it finish falling. Lay your world to rest — and carry it. (release)
[Arcana] The wardstone can give its Weave back — not embalm the dead, but feed the living.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
...I can't. I can't be the hand that ends it twice. (She seals the wardstone, and the shard stops falling — a snowflake caught mid-air, forever.)

They're safe now. Frozen, but safe; mine to keep. Don't say it. I know what it is — a tomb I'll visit and call a home, a mistake I refused to carry because clutching it was easier. I am not ready to let the sky finish. Maybe I never will be. But you stood here while I wasn't brave. That is its own kind of saving.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
No. A thing held out of its ending isn't saved — it's only made to fall forever, and I have asked enough people to fall forever. (She lifts her hand from the wardstone, and gently, the last of Netheril lets go.)

It's done. It fell. It was always going to fall, and I have spent a thousand years pretending my grief was a calculation I could redo. It isn't. Some mistakes you don't erase. You *carry* them. I think — I think I can carry this now. Because I'm not carrying it alone.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
Wait — *wait.* The wardstone isn't a cage, it's a held breath. The Weave inside it isn't spent, it's *saved* — the last live magic in the world, and I have been treating it as a shroud. I can open my own hand. Let it go not down, but *out* — back into the dead Weave of the present, a seed instead of a tomb.

There's a notation under my old proof in a hand I almost know — it says the same thing, as if someone left me the answer to find. Netheril doesn't get to live again. But it gets to *give.* My people end as the thing that makes new magic possible. That's not erasing the mistake. That's the mistake, finally, building something.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
Don't — don't hand me an elegant third door. Elegance is what I trusted last time, and it dropped a civilization out of the sky. There's no clever proof that makes this not hurt. There's only what I'm willing to carry.

So, plainly: do I freeze them and keep the tomb, or do I let the sky finish falling?

choices.text: Keep them. Freeze the shard. (preserve)
Let it fall. Lay it to rest. (release)

nodes.text: You fall out of your own century. Gold light. Sunlight on glass. A city — an entire city — floating in an impossible blue sky, held aloft by magic so total it has forgotten it is magic at all.

And then — all at once, everywhere, like a bell rung underwater — every spell in the world goes silent. The light dies. And the city, which flies by magic, begins, very slowly and then all at once, to FALL.

nodes.speaker: The Returned
nodes.text: Karsus's Folly. The day the goddess of magic died. I'm standing inside the first apocalypse — and the floor is going out from under the world. Move. MOVE.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: You pulled me from the rubble. Impossible — the ward-stones should have rendered me to ash. Unless... you are not of this Now at all. You have the look of someone *falling through time.* I have read of people like you. In the margins of a forbidden proof. In a hand identical to my own — written centuries before I was born.

choices.text: Whose hand?
No time. The city's falling — come with me, NOW.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Yours, I rather think. Which should be impossible, and is the single most interesting thing that has ever happened to me, and we are both about to die discussing it. Get me off this dying enclave and I will tell you everything I have read about you. About what you are.

choices.text: Deal. Stay close.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Then I am yours, impossible one. Mind the gaps in the floor — gravity has only just remembered it has opinions. Let us not be where the marble is not.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: I have done the impossible arithmetic of my own life ten thousand times, and never once did it let me back *here* — the hour it fell, the air still warm with the Weave before Karsus reached too far. That balcony. I derived my first proof on that balcony. It is about to be sky.

choices.text: We can't stop the fall. But you're not watching it alone this time.
Is there anything here you need to see?

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(There is no speaker. The voice is the architecture itself — a sound like a million held breaths.)* Here is the place kept for you. Read the niche. The name is half-formed; the mortar has not set. You were always going to be here. The only surprise is that you are reading it with eyes.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(Your own name is being spoken somewhere behind you, over a body that is yours — and one word in it you cannot hold. It slides off the mind like water off glass.)*

choices.text: "Whose name is that?"
[ARCANA 12] "This isn't a punishment. It's a filing system."
"I don't belong here."

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: Yours. It has always been yours. The word you cannot keep is the one that makes you a person instead of a vacancy. Someone is saying it now. Someone will forget it. *(A long, patient silence.)* So will you.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(For the first time the voice seems almost to attend to you.)* ...A cold way to put it. A true one. We are the souls no ledger wanted. Filed under nothing. You read quickly, for the newly dead. *(beat)* You will need that.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: Doubt is the crime, little vacancy. You will learn the punishment by living inside it. Read your niche. Wait your turn.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(The held breath of a million almost laughs.)* No one belongs here. That is the entire point. Belonging was the thing you all lacked. *(The mortar at the edge of your name has not set.)* ...And yet yours is the one that did not take. Curious. Hold that thought. Something else has hold of it too.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(Far off, a hand on your true chest. A pull, like a hook behind the navel. The Wall recedes — unwilling, for the first time in its existence, to let one go.)*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(Grey robes, a grave-warden's lantern, a face that has buried more people than it has loved.)* Be still. You stopped breathing on this slab a quarter-hour past. I closed your eyes myself. And now they are open, and Kelemvor's seal on your soul is — *(she falters, a priest losing her certainty)* — torn. Cleanly. From the inside.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: I have served the Lord of the Dead forty years. I have never once seen a thing come back. So I will ask plainly, and your answer decides whether I help you up or put you down: do you know what you are?

choices.text: "I saw the Wall. My name is in it."
"I don't know. But something pulled me back."
[INSIGHT 13] "You're not afraid of me. You're afraid you've been lied to for forty years."
"Put me down, then. If you can."

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(She goes very still.)* The Wall of the Faithless. You saw it from the inside and walked out the wrong way. *(She helps you sit, and her hands are shaking, and she is ashamed of it.)* Then the doctrine I have preached over a thousand graves is at best incomplete. I would rather know that than be comfortable. Come. The Cinderhaunt is no place to be newly alive.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Something. *(dry)* In my experience, child, "something" with the strength to unmake Kelemvor's own seal is rarely benign. We will find out together what has its hand on you. On your feet. The harvesters will have heard your heart restart.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(The lantern trembles, just once.)* ...You read the living as easily as the dead read you. Yes. If you are real — and you are insultingly warm for a corpse — then someone has been wrong for a very long time, and I have helped them be wrong. I intend to make that right. Starting with getting you out of here. Move.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(A grim flicker that might, in better light, be respect.)* No. I don't think I can. And a thing I cannot put down is a thing I had best understand. Walk with me, Returned. The exit is through the harvest-floor, and the harvest is not finished.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Spare me the cold-reading, corpse. Better confessors than you have tried. *(She offers a hand anyway.)* Up. Questions later. Knives now.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Stay behind me until you remember how your own legs work. When the Returned come — and they will — strike for the joints, not the heart. They have no more use for hearts than you did, an hour ago.

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: *(A masked acolyte of the Faithless Choir, frozen at the cellar mouth, staring at you like a sum that won't add.)* No. No, you're — I sealed that door. I read the rites. You were *filed.* They don't — *(his voice cracks behind the mask)* — they never come back. Not once. Not in all the years.

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: He'll want to know. Gods help me, he'll *want* you. *(He backs toward the stair, hands raised.)* I'm not your enemy. I'm not — we're trying to *end* the Wall, do you understand? The man who runs us watched it take his own daughter. If you are what you are, then you are the thing he has prayed twenty years to find.

choices.text: "Who is he?"
[PERSUADE 14] "Then take me to him. No tricks."
"I'm no one's miracle. Run."

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: Aldric Morn. *(He says it the way some say a saint's name and others say a curse — and isn't sure which he means.)* The Heretic of the Lower City. Forty years a Doomguide, until he stopped believing the dead were being kept and started believing they were being *thrown away.* Find a chandler's shop with a kettle always on. He'll be expecting you. He's been expecting you for twenty years.

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: *(He hesitates, then nods, fast.)* ...Yes. Yes, all right. But not like this, not bleeding, not with a Doomguide of Kelemvor at your shoulder — *(he glances at Garrow)* — she'll have us both in cells. Go up, go quiet, find the kettle. Tell him the herald sent you. Tell him... tell him it's true. After all this time, it's *true.*

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: *(Something in him sags with relief and grief at once.)* ...Maybe you're right. Maybe a miracle should get to choose. *(He turns to flee, then stops.)* His name is Aldric Morn. If you ever want to know what you are — that's the man who's spent his life asking. For what it's worth. For whatever you are.

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: *(He's already gone up the stair, three steps at a time, shouting a name into the dark above — Aldric, Aldric, it's true — and the word follows you out of the Cinderhaunt like a hook already set.)*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(quietly, beside you)* Aldric Morn. I knew him, Returned. Before. He was the best of us. *(She looks at the stair the herald fled up, and her grave-warden's face is, for once, simply afraid.)* Which is exactly why I want to reach him before he reaches you.

nodes.speaker: Tally (a one-eyed storyteller)
nodes.text: —and the hero died in the first chapter and came back *wrong.* Oh! Speak of the margin. Sit. I've a pouch for you and a question. The pouch first; you'll want it for the question. I keep a little vault between the pages — ten pedestals, a brazier, and the best company you'll ever lose a riddle to. Care to play?

choices.text: Show me your Vault of Tens.
Who ARE you?
Maybe later.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: A storyteller. A gambler. A note someone left in the margin of a very long book and forgot to erase. Which of those is true? *All of them, on a good day.* Now — the Vault?

choices.text: Lead on.
Not yet.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: Wonderful. Mind the step between the pages — it's a long way down to the footnotes.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: Mm. I'll be here. I'm always here. That's rather the whole tragedy of me.

nodes.speaker: Tally (between the pages)
nodes.text: Fancy meeting you here, time-walker. A quick one — no vault required: I'll not speak till I'm spoken to, and then I always have the last word. Mock me from a mountain, and you're mocked in your own voice, unheard. What am I?

choices.text: An echo.
A shadow.
Silence.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: Mm. You'll fight a roomful of those, later — wearing the faces you love. Keep the answer. Keep the warning. I'll be three pages ahead, as ever.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: Ha — no. But you *tried*, and trying amuses me almost as much as winning. Almost. Off you go.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text:
This is the safehouse the cipher named. Harper-warded, supposedly. But the wards are cold, the door's off the latch, and there's no countersign chalked on the step.

Someone I care about ran this house. I told you I had a person I'd pull from the Wall. I didn't tell you she's my sister. Wrenna. And I didn't tell you she's not in the Wall yet.

choices.text: Why lie about it?
Then let's get her out. Lead on.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Because if you knew I had something to lose, you'd know exactly where to cut me. Habit. The Harpers train it into you young, and family trains it younger.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Doomguide sign on the lintel — fresh. Kelemvor's people have been here, and they don't leave witnesses, only verdicts. Knives out. Quietly.

nodes.speaker: Wrenna Alleywind
nodes.text:
Roen. Gods — Roen, you have to go, now, before they— 

(She stops. Looks at you. Something in her face folds shut.) 

...You brought the Returned here. To my house. Do you have any idea what they'd give to have this one on an altar?

choices.text: "They." The Doomguides. You've been talking to them.
Wrenna — what did you do?

nodes.speaker: Wrenna Alleywind
nodes.text: They had a writ with your name on it, Roen. The Wall, for 'consorting with the Returned.' I had a choice: hand them Harper routes, names, drops — or watch my brother mortared into nothing while I lived. So I lied. To the Harpers, to you, to everyone. For two years.

choices.text: (Say nothing. The Doomguides are coming.)

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: ...Two years. You sold the Harpers to keep me breathing, and let me think I owed *you* nothing. We'll have that fight later. Right now there are six Doomguides between her and the door. Decide who you are after we live.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text:
The cell's down. The safehouse is ours — what's left of it. And Wrenna's standing here a traitor to the Harpers and the only family I've got, in the same body. 

I can't make this call clean. Help me make it.

choices.text: She did it to save you. Take her home. (forgive)
She betrayed the Harpers. The rules exist for a reason. (condemn)
[Persuasion] Neither. We turn her two years of lies on the Doomguides.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text:
Yeah. Yeah — come here, you idiot. (He pulls her in.) We'll explain it to the Harpers together, and if they don't like it, I know every alley out of this city.
